In practice, increases in reviewable premiums are rare on traditional health insurance due to customer resistance and administrative complexity.

key_terms.definition: Premium the insurer has the right to change at periodic intervals (typically every 5 years) to reflect updated experience in claims, expenses, or other assumptions.
Healthier policyholders lapsing after a premium increase, leaving a higher-risk remaining pool and potentially causing a spiral of further premium increases.
An expectation built up by policyholders (and sometimes upheld by regulators) that terms will not be changed, arising if premiums have not been reviewed for a long period.

exam_qa.q: Explain the practical difficulties of implementing reviewable premiums on traditional (non-unit-linked) health insurance policies.
exam_qa.a: The premium increase required may be unacceptable to policyholders who do not understand the calculation or the reasons for the increase, and who may not trust the insurer. If premiums have not been reviewed for many years, a reasonable expectation that they will not increase may have developed. Selective lapsing may follow an increase — healthier policyholders leave, worsening the risk pool. Cross-subsidies in the original premium structure may also remain. The revised premium is difficult to calculate correctly in practice.

subsections.title: Complexity of Benefit Design
bullets: The unit-linked environment permits complex benefit design with optional coverages, even within a single product type. Computer systems apply the appropriate charges from premiums and unit funds according to the chosen policy structure.

A good example is the wide range of unit-linked LTCI products launched, offering flexibility in: investment choice; level of protection; level of investment; extent of guarantees (e.g., enhanced protection from age 85).
Unit-linking also allows product types to be combined easily via riders: CI insurance riders to flexible endowment or whole-of-life products; LTCI riders to unit-linked CI or life insurance products.

subsections.title: Customer Understanding
bullets: Most customers are either unaware of the unit-linked basis or cannot understand the concepts. For those who are aware, it may create an expectation of stock-market-type investment returns and may lead them to expect a surrender value on lapse — which may not be available on particular products.

Why a surrender value may not be available on a unit-linked IP insurance contract: IP is sold primarily for protection (against disability), not investment — a surrender value adds little appeal; offering surrender values encourages early lapsing, which generates losses if the asset share is below the surrender value; premiums are set just sufficient to cover expense and morbidity charges, so the unit fund at lapse will be low relative to IP benefits; the asset share (allowing for actual expenses incurred) may be low or even negative; hence the surrender value, if offered, should be small or nil to avoid losses on lapse.
If no surrender value is offered, any lapses where the asset share is positive will be a source of profit to the insurer, allowing for lower and more attractive premium levels.

key_terms.definition: The accumulation of premiums paid, less charges and expenses, plus investment return — representing the insurer's actual accumulated fund for the policy. May be low or negative for protection-type unit-linked policies.
The amount paid to a policyholder on early termination of a long-term policy; may be zero or very low on unit-linked IP or protection products.

exam_qa.q: Suggest why a surrender value may not be available on a unit-linked IP insurance contract.
exam_qa.a: IP is sold primarily for protection against disability, not as an investment, so a surrender value adds little appeal. Offering one would encourage early lapsing, generating losses if the asset share is below the surrender value. Premiums are set just sufficient to cover expenses and morbidity charges, so the unit fund at lapse will be relatively low compared with IP benefit values, and the asset share may be low or even negative. Hence the surrender value that should be offered to avoid losses is small or zero. Where no surrender value is offered, any lapses (with a positive asset share) generate profit for the insurer, enabling lower and more attractive premiums.

4.summary:
Other customers’ needs
• Consider the needs of the intermediary (or adviser / salesperson) including policy wording, pricing, underwriting and remuneration.
• Microinsurance products provide limited benefits in return for very low premiums to those on low incomes. Provides some financial protection to those who would otherwise not be able to afford it, protecting savings and reducing the use of expensive money-lenders.

Interaction with State provision
• The State may provide sick pay, maternity / paternity pay, longer-term sickness / disability income and financial support for carers.
• Benefits offered by the State affect the level of consumer need for insurance. Government actions may directly affect health insurance providers through regulations, taxation and communication.
• Health insurers can be viewed as competing against or complementing State benefits. If complementary benefits, tax incentives may encourage self-provision.
• State healthcare systems are under pressure from the ‘demographic time-bomb’.

Higher order risks
• Risks specific to each product, including risks relating to claim inception and termination rates (IP insurance and LTCI) or the rates of diagnosis (for CI insurance), eg anti-selection and selective withdrawals; investment risk (for LTCI) and lack of control over benefit payments (for PMI).
• The insurer needs to be able to understand, measure and model the risks involved (eg for pricing or setting reserves). The credibility and availability of data is crucial.
• Anti-selection and moral hazard (including fraud and non-disclosure) must be quantified and controlled.
• Regulation / legislative changes can have retrospective impacts on in-force business.
• Guarantees (premiums, charges or benefits) and options must be adequately costed, usually using stochastic techniques.
• Financial effects of strategic decisions must be carefully modelled, taking into account the insurer’s circumstances.

Bundling and unbundling
• The preferences of the customer, broker, pricing actuary, administrator and the insurer need to be considered. These may change over time.
• A bundled package may be convenient but may not meet a customer’s needs as precisely as separate products.
• Cost savings can be made on bundled products (eg reduced administrative costs, overlaps removed) but ‘shopping around’ for separate products may be cheaper.
• Brokers aim to meet customer requirements, but may prefer the unbundled approach to keep contact with a wider range of insurers.
• Bundling spreads risk over several products and so offset risks and margins in pricing. Must allow for the way risks are combined, the possibility of anti-selection, how claims experience may differ due to bundling, the volatility of risk, the capital implications, the profitability of alternatives, regulator view and any TCF issues. Risks can be combined on an ‘either or’, or a ‘both risks’ basis.
• Bundling can create admin problems with systems.

Impact of unit-linked wrappers
• Unit-linked form shares risk with the customer.
• Easier and more acceptable to review premiums and benefits.
• Allows more complex benefit design but can be difficult for customers to understand.

19.title: Analysis of Experience

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Health and care insurers analyse experience to: help manage risk (more informed pricing and reserving), monitor actual vs expected and take corrective action, update assumptions as to future experience, monitor trends in experience, and provide management information to aid business decisions.
Areas of experience that can be analysed include: new business, lapses and renewals, morbidity / claims, mortality, claim amount, recovery, withdrawals, investment, expenses, and profits.

subsections.title: Frequency of Investigation
bullets: Analysis should be carried out on a continuous basis. Database construction must be planned carefully at outset to ensure relevant data items are available when required.
Typical frequencies by product type: PMI and health cash plans monthly; critical illness and income protection quarterly; long-term care insurance half-yearly.

PMI / health cash monthly rationale: claims are volatile (e.g. seasonal viruses); policies are short-term and renewable so trends must be spotted early to prevent anti-selection and deteriorating experience.
CI / IP quarterly rationale: morbidity claims are more predictable and less volatile; policies are longer term and changes in experience are more gradual.
LTCI half-yearly rationale: claims are stable; payments spread over the care period so adverse experience takes time to emerge; hence a longer observation period can be used.

Other factors affecting frequency: time and cost of the investigation; past variability of the experience and likelihood of future variability; quality of data available; the time period over which past experience becomes irrelevant to future experience.

exam_qa.q: Give reasons why a monthly frequency is appropriate for PMI but only half-yearly for LTCI.
exam_qa.a: PMI: claim amounts are volatile (e.g. extreme weather, virus outbreaks); policies are short-term and renewable so trends must be spotted early to prevent anti-selection. LTCI: claims are stable; payments are spread over the length of the care period so adverse experience does not affect the insurer quickly — a longer observation period is therefore appropriate.

subsections.title: Sub-division of Data
bullets: The exposure should be investigated according to: product type, gender, age, region / location, occupation, sales channel, sales individual, policy type, policy size, duration, reinsurance category (retained and ceded), member cost-sharing (e.g. excess levels), underwriting method, group size (for group business), industry (for group business).
In practice, sub-divisions available depend on data volume. Small portfolios produce sparse risk cells — the actuary must exercise judgement as to the sub-divisions used.

subsections.title: Communication of Results
bullets: Systematic monitoring reveals valuable information for underwriters, administrators, claims staff and actuaries — it is important that information is communicated to all interested parties and acted upon.

Other business areas that may benefit from monitoring results include: marketing and sales strategy, reinsurance, product development, pricing, reserving, audit, tax authorities, accounts, rating agencies, and management.

subsections.title: Mortality
bullets: Typical sub-divisions for analysing mortality: type of contract, age, gender, duration from entry (or from start of claim for sickness terminations), smoker / non-smoker status, underwriting status, benefit conditions (e.g. length of deferred period).

Under long-term products, mortality is an important component in pricing and reserving. Under accelerated CI, death triggers payment of the full sum insured. Under some LTCI policies, a death benefit is part of the contract conditions; under others, death signals a release of reserve in favour of the insurer.

Two exceptions where a sizeable release of reserves on death occurs: (1) a single premium immediate needs LTCI policy — large reserves early but small/no death benefit; mortality risk lies with the policyholder; (2) an IP or pre-funded LTCI policy where a significant claim has just gone into payment — a claims-in-payment reserve would be released on early death.
Standard exposed-to-risk techniques are used (see CS2). Only relevant deaths should be included; data should be adjusted for non-reported deaths.
IP: experience split into (a) mortality in claim and (b) mortality pre-claim — mortality of claimants is much higher than non-claimants. Likely number of deaths is small relative to health claims.
CI: even for a large portfolio, deaths may be too few to warrant extensive and regular analyses.
LTCI: portfolios tend to be small — mortality investigation compares actual deaths with expected from standard mortality tables used for pricing and reserving.
PMI: mortality can be ignored for short-term products unless a death benefit is payable, in which case it is treated as a type of benefit and actual is compared to expected.

key_terms.definition: For IP insurance, the mortality experience is split between lives already on claim (significantly higher mortality) and lives not yet claiming — important for homogeneity of analysis.

exam_qa.q: Give two exceptions to the general rule that the release of reserves on death in a health and care policy will be reasonably small.
exam_qa.a: (1) A single premium immediate needs LTCI policy: reserves are sizeable particularly towards the beginning of the contract, yet there is normally no (or a small) death benefit — the mortality risk is with the policyholder as early death leaves the insurer a large release of reserves. (2) An IP or pre-funded LTCI policy where a claim of significant size has just gone into payment: a claims-in-payment reserve is set up, but if early death occurs this would be released.

subsections.title: Morbidity Experience
bullets: Morbidity claims are investigated by incidence and claim cost. Although there are similarities in approach, each product type is considered separately.

IP incidence levels of investigation: product type, distributor / sales channel, age, gender, occupation (if known), region / area of residence, duration from entry, deferred period. Recovery rates also investigated by: duration, age, gender, incapacity type, replacement ratio.

IP portfolios are split into: (a) new claims — investigated by incidence and expected claim cost using appropriate mortality and morbidity tables; (b) existing claim portfolio — actual vs expected reviewed against the tables used by the insurer.

CI incidence: rates examined at portfolio level and subdivided similarly to PMI (excluding provider/hospital and NCD level). Cause of claim is a key factor; establishing the true cause can be difficult when several diseases are present or when the policyholder dies with an accelerated CI contract. Incidence may be analysed by severity where reduced benefits apply for lower-severity cases.

LTCI — two contract types: (a) pre-funded — investigated similarly to medical expenses; incidence and claim cost determined by contract sub-division (benefit category); (b) immediate needs annuities — no morbidity experience arises; only mortality experience assessed by comparing actual deaths with expected from impaired life mortality used in pricing.

PMI and health cash plans incidence levels of investigation: product type, distributor / sales channel, provider / hospital, medical procedure or policy benefit section, age, gender, occupation (if known), region / area of residence, duration from entry, NCD level (if appropriate), member cost-sharing (e.g. excess levels), underwriting method, group size (for group business), industry (for group business).

A broad-brush approach is the overall loss ratio (claims incurred as a percentage of premium). Simple and easy to calculate but ignores differences by rating factor and does not necessarily indicate profitability.

key_terms.definition: The rate at which new morbidity claims arise within the insured population over a given period.
Claims incurred expressed as a percentage of premium — a broad-brush measure of morbidity experience that does not necessarily indicate profitability.
No Claims Discount level — a rating factor used in PMI reflecting the claimant's prior claims history.

exam_qa.q: Why is the overall loss ratio a limited measure of claims experience?
exam_qa.a: It is very simplistic — it ignores differences in experience by rating factors and does not necessarily indicate profitability. For example, a loss ratio of 120% may still be profitable if claims are not due to be paid for some time (allowing investment income to make up the profit). Other factors such as expenses and reinsurance also affect profitability.

subsections.title: Exposed to Risk
bullets: An important principle is correspondence between numerator (claims) and denominator (exposure) in all calculations.
For all health and care insurance contracts, particular care must be taken to ensure that only lives insured in the investigation period are included, and that claims are correctly included.
Adjustments to exposed to risk are needed where life exposure only contributes to part of the investigation period — done on a life-by-life basis using dates of entry and / or exit.
Further adjustments are needed if the life insured is subject to a waiting period (an initial period where no claim can be made).

key_terms.definition: The total period of exposure of lives to the risk of a claim during the investigation period — the denominator in experience rate calculations.
An initial period after policy inception during which no claim can be made — the exposure during this period must be excluded from the denominator.

subsections.title: Claim Amount
bullets: Claim amounts, even when fixed in the contract, need monitoring. Monitoring should reveal whether there are significant differences in claim inception rates by level of sum insured — reflecting anti-selection or different underwriting levels applied at different benefit levels.

Example: an abnormally high claim frequency for CI policies with sum insured of £99,000 likely indicates that underwriting criteria change at the £100,000 mark, with brokers / customers selecting against the insurer by staying just below the threshold.

PMI / health cash (indemnity benefits): average benefit payout investigated for major diseases and procedures, sub-classified by inpatient / day case / outpatient, type of hospital, source of distribution, group or individual, geographical region, policy type, presence of pre-agreed fee schedules. New drugs / procedures may cause average costs to move upwards or downwards.
CI and IP: monitoring by claim amount detects variation in incidence rates by sum insured — identifying anti-selection.

LTCI where cash benefit: no variation by claim amount (fixed benefit). Where the benefit indemnifies actual care costs up to a limit, experience is subject to volatility — analysis by amount is crucial for profitability assessment, reserve setting and premium reassessment.

exam_qa.q: A UK health and care insurer finds an abnormally high claim frequency for CI policyholders with sum insured of £99,000. Give a possible reason.
exam_qa.a: The underwriting criteria probably change dramatically at the £100,000 sum insured mark (e.g. applicants must complete a detailed medical questionnaire or undergo a medical exam). A high frequency just below this level suggests that customers or brokers are aware of this threshold and are selecting against the insurer by applying for a slightly smaller sum insured, knowing they are a relatively bad risk for the insurer.

subsections.title: IBNR and RBNS Bases
bullets: Regular investigations must check retrospectively that IBNR (incurred but not reported) and RBNS (reported but not settled — also called outstanding claims) bases are adequate.
Under-reserving for RBNS on CI has occurred in the past. In the UK, it is estimated that 50% of ultimate CI claims cost is RBNS at end of year of diagnosis, and 10% at end of the following year.
Reasons for CI under-reserving: CI has been expanding rapidly; insurers have often made only simplistic allowances for reserves; lack of reliable information.

Why both IBNR and RBNS arise on CI — IBNR: policyholders may not know they are covered, or may be too traumatised to claim immediately (e.g. terminal cancer); the 30-day survival clause also causes delays. RBNS: settlement delays arise from claim verification and gathering of information.

key_terms.definition: Incurred But Not Reported — claims that have occurred during the investigation period but have not yet been reported to the insurer.
Reported But Not Settled (also called outstanding claims) — claims that have been reported but where the final settlement amount has not yet been paid.

exam_qa.q: Why do CI policies have both IBNR and RBNS?
exam_qa.a: IBNR: many policyholders do not realise they are covered, or the illness is so traumatic (e.g. terminal cancer) that claiming is not their immediate concern — causing reporting delays. The 30-day survival clause can also cause delays. RBNS: settlement delays occur due to the need for claim verification and gathering of necessary information.

subsections.title: Seasonality
bullets: Experience analyses must allow for seasonal effects. In most countries, there are peaks and troughs in mortality and morbidity throughout the year, even in countries with relatively benign climates.
PMI / health cash plans: considerable bias towards non-holiday times for elective acute medical procedures — by both medical specialists and policyholders.
Specialist contracts with benefits for winter sports injuries will have a specialised claim distribution.
IP: surge in claims at end of deferred period following the peak health claim time of late winter (e.g. for a 6-month deferred period, peak IP claims are at end of summer).

UK December effect: few new claims notified in late December due to Christmas post delays and low staffing levels — January shows a compensatory higher experience. IBNR assumption should be higher at year-end than at other quarter-ends.

Adjusting for seasonality — for pricing: spread seasonal claims to give an expected overall annual cost; for reserving: deal with seasonal claims explicitly (e.g. reserving on a quarterly basis) rather than spreading, which would produce too large reserves for some periods and too small for others.

subsections.title: Reinsurance
bullets: Under a quota share arrangement, the claims experience for the reinsured section is identical to that of the total portfolio (each claim is split in the same proportions). The overall profitability of the quota share contract will differ due to different expenses, commissions and risk premiums.
Under surplus reinsurance, the proportion ceded can differ for each individual contract — so the pure claims experience for the cedant and reinsurer will differ.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance arrangement where each claim is split between cedant and reinsurer in fixed proportions — the claims experience of both parties is identical.
Reinsurance arrangement where the proportion ceded varies by individual contract — the pure claims experience of cedant and reinsurer will therefore differ.

sections.title: The Effect of Early Claims, Non-Disclosure and Anti-Selection

subsections.title: The Effect on Claims Experience
bullets: Anti-selection: the general effect of potential policyholders using information not available to the insurer to take out cover on favourable terms. People who think they are more likely to claim tend to be those who take out policies. Anti-selection can be reasonable if the insurer does not ask for sufficient information to accurately determine an applicant's risk.
Non-disclosure: the specific circumstance where the applicant is asked to give the insurer details of information relevant to the risk, but fails to disclose such information.
Both lead to early claims — policyholders claiming sooner than the insurer would normally expect.

Two purposes for investigation: (1) at the onset of a claim — identify cause and spot trends (e.g. epidemics) early so action can be taken; (2) general monitoring at set intervals — identify more gradual trends (e.g. decrease in claims costs due to medical advances).

These effects may be seen across the entire portfolio or concentrated in specific regions, sales channels, individual sales, policy type or policy size. In the UK, anti-selection has been seen in PMI and LTCI policies.

Durational effect: impact of early claims, non-disclosure and anti-selection is concentrated in the first year of insurance, and to a certain extent the second year. After that, other factors dominate and experience reverts to normal.

In the early years of CI being written in the UK, many early claims arose from some sales channels due to non-disclosure encouraged by over-enthusiastic salespeople. With increasing insurer scrutiny and litigation, this poor sales technique is now in decline.

key_terms.definition: The general effect of potential policyholders using private information (not available to the insurer) to take out cover on favourable terms — high-risk individuals are more likely to purchase cover.
The specific circumstance where an applicant is asked to provide information relevant to the risk assessment but fails to disclose such information.
A claim arising sooner than the insurer would normally expect, typically resulting from anti-selection or non-disclosure.

exam_qa.q: Explain the distinction between anti-selection and non-disclosure.
exam_qa.a: Anti-selection is the general effect of potential policyholders using information that is not available to the insurer — taking out cover on favourable terms. It can be reasonable if the insurer does not ask for sufficient information. Non-disclosure occurs in the specific circumstance where the applicant is asked to give the insurer details of information relevant to the risk, but fails to disclose such information — this is not reasonable and may lead to the policy being voided.

exam_qa.q: Why will anti-selection in LTCI normally only be a problem for pre-funded products?
exam_qa.a: Anti-selection is more of a problem for pre-funded products because the insurer is insuring both the incidence and duration of care. In contrast, immediate needs policyholders are already in a state of requiring long-term care — the insurer is only insuring the duration of care (not the incidence), so anti-selection on incidence does not apply.

subsections.title: Estimating the Impact
bullets: The impact of anti-selection, non-disclosure and early claims is difficult to estimate. The actuary first compares actual claims against expected at early durations — though the divergence may simply reflect inaccuracy or irrelevance of the original pricing data.

The actuary next compares company experience with market experience (where incidence data by duration is available), adjusting for differing policy conditions and target market. Statistics from overseas or the general population may be helpful but must be treated with caution.
On non-disclosure and anti-selection, the actuary examines the underwriting questions asked and the distribution methods employed, comparing these with practices elsewhere in the marketplace.

Specific actions include: investigate early claims by cause (look for causes with excessive claims); review underwriting questions on standard proposal forms; review sums insured limits beyond which further underwriting applies; review questions on supplementary questionnaires; review methods of sale and intended target market.

subsections.title: Impact of Economic Conditions on Claims Experience
bullets: Economic conditions affect the propensity to claim under three headings: (a) adverse company conditions (employer of the lives insured); (b) general economic conditions; (c) regional economic conditions.

Adverse company conditions: a perceived threat of redundancy or downsizing causes employees to accelerate health claims (especially PMI and health cash) to get medical procedures done quickly before employment and benefits end. Claims under group insurance are made more quickly than those under individual cover.

In IP insurance, the employer may initiate a claim rather than impose redundancy — in the employer's interest to have the employee's salary paid by the insurer. Conversely, if an employer makes least healthy employees redundant first, IP claims experience temporarily improves.
IP recovery rates may fall as jobs become unavailable. Must be analysed on employer-by-employer basis; results incorporated into pricing of new group business.

General economic conditions: a general economic downturn increases claim levels due to higher morbidity from stress, poverty and uncertainty. Must be incorporated into pricing, especially group new business.
Regional economic conditions: impact greatest for specific industries. These effects must be incorporated into pricing of new group business.
Measuring the impact: compare experience of a particular period with a similar timespan when the conditions were not evident. Adjustment may be needed for inflation and other unrelated factors.

subsections.title: Inter-company Comparisons (Competition)
bullets: Health and care products vary considerably, making inter-company comparisons difficult. Insurers are possessive about claims experience data on commercial sensitivity grounds — sharing pricing and product terms might breach competition legislation.

More useful is to contribute data to formal inter-company investigations. In the UK, the Continuous Mortality Investigation (CMI) pools mortality data across companies; comparative individual company experience is not released except to each contributing insurer.
The CMI is administered by the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries. The actuary can compare internal results on a known basis with the market as represented by CMI figures.
Additional value is obtained by understanding why the experience of various insurers differs — which can often be by a substantial margin.

key_terms.definition: A UK industry-wide inter-company investigation of claims experience, pooling mortality data across individual companies and administered by the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries. Individual comparative experience is not released except to the contributing insurer.

subsections.title: The Insurance Cycle
bullets: The insurance cycle: a class of business moves from profitability to losses and back to profitability, driven by market forces of supply and demand. Despite clear historical cyclical patterns, the cycle is quite difficult to predict.
Phase 1 — High profits: high premiums, good profitability. New insurers enter and existing ones expand, attracted by good returns.

Phase 2 — Greater competition: more companies join, premiums soften (low, less profitable rates). Commissions raised, advertising increased, prices reduced. Profit margins trimmed. Often coincides with an economic upturn.
Phase 3 — Losses: reduced profits or losses; cycle goes into depression. At the bottom, insurers start to leave the market or reduce involvement.

Phase 4 — Reduced competition and start of upswing: as losses emerge and capital disappears, premium rates are generally increased. Smaller insurers may find survival difficult. Eventually rates rise to cover losses and the cycle returns to Phase 1.

The insurance / underwriting cycle is less commonly influential in long-term regular premium products (individual CI and IP) — more commonly found in short-term general insurance where business is renewed annually and rates can change more frequently.
Group IP insurance has historically featured in this cycle. Different classes will be at different points of the cycle at different times.

key_terms.definition: A cyclical pattern of insurance profitability driven by market supply and demand: high profits attract new entrants, competition softens rates, losses emerge, insurers exit, rates harden, and the cycle repeats.
Phase of the insurance cycle where premiums are low and less profitable due to high competition.

subsections.title: Actual versus Expected
bullets: Main purposes of expense analysis for a health insurer: (1) provides information to allocate expenses between different classes and rating groups; (2) estimates the sizes of different elements of expenses for use in premium rating; (3) assists with financial plans.

Expenses of health and care insurance contracts are higher than for most other insurance products due to more administration, more claims management / control and more detailed medical underwriting. Poor expense control can waste underwriting profit.

Direct expenses: those that depend on either the volume of new business or the level of in-force business. Overheads: those relating to general management and service departments — insensitive to the volume of new business or in-force level.
Five types of non-commission expenses: initial, renewal, termination, claims, and investment expenses.

The insurer monitors actual vs expected expenses on a monthly basis. More detailed investigations coincide with accounting reporting timings and include: (a) expenses allocation between fixed and variable; (b) product contributions against expenses incurred.

key_terms.definition: Expenses that depend on either the volume of new business written or the level of in-force business — they vary with business volumes.
Expenses relating to general management and service departments not directly involved in new business or policy maintenance — insensitive to the volume of business.

exam_qa.q: State the main purposes of an expense analysis for a health insurer.
exam_qa.a: (1) Provides information to allocate expenses between different classes and rating groups. (2) Estimates the sizes of the different elements of expenses for use in premium rating. (3) Assists with financial plans.

subsections.title: Expenses Allocation Between Fixed and Variable
bullets: It is advisable to collect expense information split between fixed and variable. Expenses should also be sub-divided between initial, renewal, claim, termination and investment expenses.

subsections.title: Product Contributions Against Expenses Incurred
bullets: The relationship between expenses incurred and the contributions to expenses from within each product should be examined on a regular basis. Results need to be incorporated into the pricing and reserving bases of the products.

subsections.title: Inflation Re-examined
bullets: Due allowance must be built into various health and care insurance contracts for the effect of inflation on expenses.

Main drivers of inflation on expenses: salaries (e.g. of medical practitioners and claims assessors); medical advances (e.g. those leading to earlier recovery will lead to lower associated claims handling costs); IT costs (e.g. associated with administration of claims and policies).

For PMI (one-year basis): possible to adjust for expense inflation at each renewal. For longer-term contracts (CI and IP): allowances for future expense inflation are included in the assumptions used when calculating premium rates.

Analysis must determine the extent to which expense components are price-inflation or earnings-inflation sensitive, and whether increasing costs are mirrored by national indices or require separate adjustment.

subsections.title: Treatment of Significant Individual Expenditures
bullets: For significant one-off expenditures (e.g. company acquisition, installation of new IT systems), consider spreading the allowance over a number of years.
The effect on profit reporting, capital requirements and tax must be considered. Competition may mean large costs have to be amortised over several years of new business.

subsections.title: Actual versus Expected
bullets: The insurer monitors actual investment income received (after allowing for capital movements) against the expected level.

The significance of investment income varies by contract: low for PMI (small claims reserves); very important for IP insurance (large claims reserves). The analysis is split by investment class and includes dividends, interest and capital gains (realised and unrealised), performed both gross and net of investment expenses.

subsections.title: Other Analyses
bullets: Other investment investigations include: comparison with the achievements of similar insurers in this area; assessment of the returns achieved by appointed fund managers against targets, against each other and against market indices.

subsections.title: Purpose
bullets: Movements normally monitored: new business, lapses, deaths (whether or not leading to a claim), disability claims, endorsements, mid-term cancellations.

Endorsements: additions / changes made to a policy after it has incepted (e.g. adding a child to a family policy or increasing benefits). Mid-term cancellations: policies that cancel at a time other than renewal.

By studying movements and their trends, an insurer can: measure the extent to which different parts of the portfolio are growing or contracting; get an early indication of undue losses or gains (rates out of line with market); assess the effects of a new set of rates or a marketing campaign on the portfolio; validate the assumptions used to derive the existing premium rates.
Movement analyses give an important early warning of adverse changes — done at least monthly.

key_terms.definition: An addition or change made to a policy after it has incepted — for example, adding a child to a family policy or increasing benefit levels.
A policy that cancels at a time other than renewal.

subsections.title: Calculation of Movement Rates
bullets: Movement rates (lapse rate, etc.) are expressed by reference to a suitable measure of exposure using the principle of correspondence — the denominator should form the total exposure corresponding to the movements counted in the numerator.
Use movements actually taking place in the period, not those reported in the period — reporting and processing delays mean the two are not the same.

Key formulae: lapse rate (short-term) = no. lapses for period x divided by no. renewals invited for period x. Cancellation rate = no. cancellations during period x divided by no. policies exposed for period x. New business rate = no. new policies for period x divided by no. renewals invited for period x.

Problems with delays: delays between movement happening and computer record being updated are the main problem. Three possible solutions: (a) wait until all movements are on the system — not desirable as it delays early warning; (b) speed up processing — desirable but not fully practical; (c) make intelligent estimates of ultimate numbers based on initial numbers using past experience of processing speeds — the likely accepted compromise.

Lapses (long-term business): numbers lapsing compared to portfolio exposed to risk to produce lapse rates. A key area is duration from entry — susceptibility to loss measured by whether the capital outlay in writing the policy has been repaid by premiums received prior to lapse.
Relative lapse rates by sub-group: used to monitor differences between sub-groups relating to distribution channels, geographical area, policy size, benefit type, yearly cohorts.

The volume and distribution of lapses should be examined with investigations into source and type of lapse, comparing against the lapse basis assumed by the insurer. Attempts should be made to ascertain whether a policy lapse is genuine or a non-reported death. Persistency and profitability by source is important to health and care insurers.
The analysis of new business and lapse by distribution type provides a key indication of profit generated by each form of sales medium and feeds into future company sales and marketing strategy.

key_terms.definition: The denominator (exposure) used in a rate calculation should correspond exactly to the population that generates the events counted in the numerator.
For short-term business: number of lapses in a period divided by number of renewals invited in that period.

subsections.title: Example Calculation of Lapse Rates
bullets: To calculate lapse rates, a basis for estimating notification and processing delays for lapses is needed. Example: proportions of lapses processed by end of month 0 (renewal month), 1, 2, 3, 4 are 37%, 68%, 89%, 97%, 100%.

Example (April lapse rate, analysis at start of June): April is at end of lapse development month 1 (68% processed). With 177 lapses processed so far, estimated total = 177 / 0.68 = 260. With 880 renewals invited, lapse rate = 260 / 880 = 30%.

A sudden jump in lapse rates from February onwards in an analysis may indicate: (a) a change in how lapses are processed (invalidating the estimation basis); or (b) a sudden deterioration in the competitiveness of premium rates (own rate increase in February, or competitor rate reduction).
An increase in lapses is not necessarily bad — it might reflect a strategic decision to write fewer policies at higher premium margins.

The total number of lapses actually processed in a given month can be estimated by summing contributions from each prior month weighted by the incremental processing proportions for each development month.

subsections.title: Purposes
bullets: A portfolio analysis is an investigation of the risks covered, broken down into various levels by grouping policies of a similar nature.

Purposes include: to give basic information on the amount of business being written; to provide evidence that premium rates for particular rating cells are out of line with the rest of the market; to show where the company is over-exposed to particular types of risk (allowing corrective action e.g. via reinsurance or ceasing to write new business for those risks); to provide information for planning and budgeting exercises.

subsections.title: Portfolio Analysis Between Classes
bullets: At its simplest, the analysis splits business by class of insurance (CI, IP, LTCI, PMI) and possibly by group or individual. Shows number of policies in force and total premium income, each shown as a percentage of all business.
The gross premium at the last renewal date (or policy inception date for new policies) is typically used.

Problems: (1) analysis based on numbers of policies contains wide variation in size of risk per policy from one class to another; (2) where some policies are non-annual, analysis at a given date can be misleading; (3) delays in processing new policies, lapses and cancellations mean data at a given date will not accurately reflect the true in-force position.
More useful than comparing classes is to compare performance of variations within a product (e.g. experience tabulated by hospital grouping for PMI contracts).

subsections.title: Portfolio Analysis by Risk Group
bullets: A more detailed analysis breaks down the portfolio within a class by risk group, using numbers of policies and amounts of premium. An analysis based on the exposure measure for the class can break experience into comparable risk units.
Single-factor analysis (one-way table): each rating factor is considered individually (e.g. for PMI by hospital band). Simple but does not capture interactions between factors.

Multi-factor analysis: several rating factors combined in a single table (e.g. age band, gender, excess level and hospital band for PMI). More precise — removes distortions caused by correlations between rating factors. Downside: cells must be grouped into broad bands to avoid unmanageable numbers with sparse data.
A full portfolio analysis for a major class (e.g. PMI) would include one-way tables for every rating factor plus various multi-factor tables combining the major rating factors.

key_terms.definition: Portfolio analysis where each rating factor is considered individually — simple but does not capture interactions between factors.
Portfolio analysis where several rating factors are combined in a single table — removes distortions from correlations between factors but requires grouping into broad bands to maintain credibility.

19.summary:
Analysis of experience

• Carried out at intervals suited to the product and the data available. The subdivision of data depends on the volume of data available and the product.
• The following factors will be investigated: mortality; morbidity; claim amount.
• Consideration given to: the adequacy of IBNR and RBNS provisions; seasonality; reinsurance; the economy; inter-company comparisons; the insurance cycle.

Anti-selection

• Must pay particular attention to the effect of early claims, non-disclosure and anti-selection.
• Measuring the impact of these can be difficult.

Expenses

• Expense control is important.
• Actual expenses compared to those expected, splitting fixed and variable, initial, renewal, claim, termination and investment.
• Allowances made for expense inflation and any significant individual expenditure.

Investment income

• Actual investment income compared to that expected.
• Comparisons made with returns made by competitors, targets and market indices.

Analysis of movements

• The following movements may be monitored: new business; lapses; deaths; disability claims; endorsements; mid-term cancellations.
• An insurer will monitor movement rates to: measure a portfolio’s growth and contraction; get an early indication of undue losses or gains; assess the effects of a new set of rates or marketing campaign; validate premium rates.

Portfolio analysis

• Useful for: giving basic information about the quantity of business written; premium rating; assessing exposure to types of risk; planning and budgeting.
• The level of detail depends on the purpose of the investigation, multi-factor analyses can be used if data allows.

21.title: Assessing Strategies

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Assessment involves much more than just a valuation — it requires consideration of many different factors leading to a recommended course of action (e.g. a solvency assessment considers factors affecting asset/liability values, reasons for current position, future expected position, and recommends action).
The value an actuary adds lies in the assessment of business strategies (not just in crunching numbers) — this is the focus of Ch21.
Core Reading examples (new company launch, overseas markets, takeover) are illustrative — use them to develop a framework for tackling any strategic decision-making problem; do not rote learn.
Chapter structure: S1 = new company launch; S2 = overseas markets; S3 = company/portfolio takeover; S4 = data sources for all three analyses.

sections.title: Assessment of the Market for a New Company Launch

subsections.title: Consumer Demand
bullets: For each product, need to set premium rates ensuring required profit given available capital and risk — requires a financial model of expected cashflows.

Key model inputs: market volumes and policyholder characteristics. Characteristics affect: (i) level of cover (e.g. CI sum insured, IP deferred period); (ii) price charged (via rating factors); (iii) claims experience (incidence and average cost); (iv) option take-up rates; (v) lapse/renewal/mid-year cancellation rates; (vi) acquisition expenses.
Need volume breakdown by distribution channel and customer characteristics at sufficient detail.
Products must meet a real consumer need and be affordable; how much a customer will pay depends on product type, how it is sold, and how well it meets their needs.
Methods to assess whether products meet consumer needs: market research (surveys, interviews, focus groups), views of intermediaries/sales staff, analysis of demand for competitors' similar products.

Brand image important: presenting as 'insurance company' projects financial strength but is affected by industry-wide image; presenting as 'healthcare company' makes customer service reputation paramount. New providers must build brand from scratch — often start in niche markets; may leverage parent company name.
Distribution channel choice directly affects profitability — impacts sales volumes, expenses, persistency and claims experience.
Market share estimation: (a) estimate total market × company market share; OR (b) maximum potential demand × expected take-up rate × company market share.
Level of competition affects both (i) expected total market volumes and (ii) market share achievable by any one company.

exam_qa.q: Why is it important to determine the characteristics of future policyholders?
exam_qa.a: Characteristics affect: level of cover taken (CI sum insured, IP deferred period); price charged (rating factors); claims experience (incidence and average cost); option take-up rates; lapse/renewal/cancellation rates; and acquisition expenses (may be higher for certain customer segments).

exam_qa.q: What methods can a new insurer use to assess the extent to which its products will meet customer needs?
exam_qa.a: Market research (surveys, interviews, focus groups); ascertaining views of intermediaries and sales staff (interviews, questionnaires); looking at competitors and assessing demand for similar products.

subsections.title: Other Launch Considerations
bullets: Profitability of existing players: indicator of own profit potential; details often confidential but published accounts, supervisory reports, and consultants can help. Note: other companies' profits are only one factor — profit can be measured many ways and more detailed analysis is needed.

Capital needs for new product/company launch: (i) launch expenses (product design, marketing, training, systems); (ii) expense overruns in early years (staff inexperience); (iii) new business strain (initial expenses + reserves + solvency capital > first premium). Additional regulatory capital may apply for start-up operations.

New companies are small → proportionately higher capital requirements than large companies; greater risks (e.g. lack of relevant data) → higher regulatory capital requirements. Must assess access to sufficient capital.

Data: more significant issue for new company than for existing company launching a new product. Factors affecting data relevance: differences in class of lives, benefit/policy conditions, underwriting and claims management, distribution methods, culture/religion/attitudes/legislation (especially for overseas data), and base period (currency of data).
Data must also be credible — sufficient volume required.
Administration: new staff and systems required rather than just retraining/adapting; key roles include sales, underwriters, admin and claims staff.

exam_qa.q: Why is capital needed to launch a new product?
exam_qa.a: Capital needed for: (i) launch expenses (product design, marketing, training, systems); (ii) expense overruns in early years due to staff inexperience; (iii) new business strain — initial expenses, reserves, and required solvency capital likely to exceed first premium. Additional regulatory capital requirements may apply for start-up operations.

exam_qa.q: List the factors that need to be considered in determining the relevance of data to a product.
exam_qa.a: Differences in: (i) class of lives (e.g. age, socio-economic group); (ii) benefits and policy conditions; (iii) underwriting and claims management; (iv) distribution methods; (v) culture, religion, attitudes to claiming, legislation (especially for overseas data); (vi) base period — how up-to-date the data are. Volume must also be sufficient for credibility.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Health and care insurance is highly localised — each country, and sometimes each region, must be approached as a separate operation.
Cashflow model required; data and assumptions must allow for local variations.

Criteria for identifying overseas opportunities: (i) contracts where insurer has specialist knowledge; (ii) superior claims control system vs local competitors; (iii) links with a key local distributor; (iv) product fits target market needs and is affordable.
Key issues to consider: level/type of competition, State provision, distribution channels, local representation, culture, regulation/legal, reinsurance.

subsections.title: Level and Type of Competition
bullets: Competition varies widely: large domestic insurers, State quasi-monopolies, multinationals, local third-party administrators (TPAs), community self-funding groups, or nothing at all.
India: health insurance available through four large State-owned insurers or private stand-alone health insurers (SAHIs), often joint ventures between local and large overseas insurers.
China: highly competitive, large domestic insurers dominant, growing foreign presence.
Rwanda: community-funded 'Mutuelle de Santé' covers virtually the entire population.

Ireland example: VHI monopoly until 1994; community rating plus risk equalisation mechanism; 2008 court ruling deemed risk equalisation unfair; government introduced insurance levy to fund tax relief for older customers.
Local TPAs dominate Middle East and some African markets — specialist local knowledge invaluable.
Actuary must be wary of markets where there is no public or private pre-funded health provision; local research needed to understand reasons.

subsections.title: State Provision
bullets: Level of State provision directly impacts demand for PMI and also influences other H&C products.
Developed countries: State may play important role; range for private insurance can vary considerably.
Under-developed countries: State provision at low level; only wealthy and expatriates can afford private healthcare.
Sweden example: high quality State healthcare + no tax breaks for private insurance → only ~1% of population have private cover.
Private facilities may be worse than public in some countries (limiting PMI demand), or better in others.

subsections.title: Distribution Channels Available
bullets: Distribution channels vary by country; insurer must understand local sales remuneration structures and any regulation of sales.
Full sales training and tight control of sales staff often necessary; local representatives and reinsurers invaluable.
International PMI: possible to sell in one country for coverage in another (e.g. expat cover); ability to do so depends on countries involved.
EU-based insurers can sell in any other EU country without additional authorisation.

subsections.title: Local Representation and Assistance
bullets: Local presence is essential — all literature must be translated; joint venture with a local insurer may be needed.
Key local contacts include hospitals and doctors/consultants; reinsurer or consultancy firm can facilitate introductions.

Areas where local expertise helps: understanding local market/competition/State provision/regulation; understanding local culture; establishing distribution channels; technical assistance in product design; establishing agreements with local medical providers; providing local data.
If joint venture not possible: consider takeover of local insurer or rely on reinsurer assistance.
Overseas takeover generally much riskier than domestic takeover — uncertainties described in S3 are much greater for overseas business.
Local data needed for: pricing, and for cashflow models assessing viability (expected claims, sales volumes, price levels).

subsections.title: Culture
bullets: Customer expectations for insurance products and benefits vary considerably between countries.
EU regulation: insurers must charge same prices to men and women — no gender distinction permitted.
UAE example: PMI maternity benefits only available for married women.
Religious views can affect healthcare organisation and delivery; e.g. Sharia-compliant insurance products available in some countries.
Insurer wary of linking claim admission to State scheme admission — public criteria far less rigorous than private insurer's → higher claims.
UK PMI tie to NHS: (i) referral from claimant's own GP required; (ii) 'six week plans' — claim only accepted if NHS waiting list exceeds specified time.

UK policy conditions may not be applicable in other territories: e.g. GP referral may be impractical; 'six week rule' may be inappropriate and difficult to administer; doctors may refer a much greater proportion of patients privately.

exam_qa.q: Why cannot an insurer simply apply the same policy conditions and claims control for overseas PMI as used in the UK?
exam_qa.a: Policy conditions similar to UK may be unacceptable (e.g. GP referral condition where seeing a doctor is expensive); may be difficult to justify excluding someone for private treatment already accepted for public (with long waiting list); 'six week rule' impractical in some territories; referral patterns of local doctors may be very different (much greater proportion referred privately), making the impact of restrictions very different from UK.

subsections.title: Regulation and Legal Matters
bullets: Detailed knowledge of local legislation/regulation required, AND how these rules are interpreted and used in practice.
Contracts must be subject to local law; local representation vital to ensure contracts are interpreted and effected as intended.

subsections.title: Reinsurance
bullets: Reinsurers can provide essential assistance in establishing overseas health insurance markets: assessing local-specific risks, providing data required to model viability.
Cross-reference: Chapter 17 for more detail on reinsurer assistance.

subsections.title: Example: Microinsurance
bullets: Target market: working people on low incomes (typically <$8/day) in developing countries.

Opportunities: new market access and profits; huge potential market (4 billion people on low incomes); wider social benefit (part of ethical strategy); grants may be available from development funds and governments to support microinsurance initiatives.

Target market characteristics: may lack bank accounts; short-term planning horizons; manage risks through informal/social networks; mobile phone coverage enables digital distribution at affordable cost; limited familiarity with formal insurance → risk of misunderstanding coverage; financial literacy often low (companies collaborate with regulators on financial education; pictures and acting used in low-literacy areas); may distrust insurance companies and believe insurance is only for the rich.

Launch considerations: overseas launch → Sections 2.2–2.8 relevant. Main issues are pricing and profitability: risks specific to local market; limited suitable data → difficult to set premium/benefit levels accurately; design must be kept simple.
Low premium/benefit → low margins per policy → high sales volumes essential; may take years to reach profitable scale.
Distribution and premium collection more difficult and expensive than traditional insurance → low-cost operating model vital.
Mobile phone companies used to sell insurance and collect premiums at top-up — efficient as mobile coverage often 10× greater than insurance penetration.
Challenges: recording member/claims information; 'normal' insurance definitions may not apply (e.g. definition of household); coping with huge volumes of small policies.
key_terms: microinsurance: insurance for low-income working populations in developing countries, characterised by low premiums, low benefits, simple design, and high volumes

sections.title: Assessment of a Company or Portfolio for Takeover

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Types of takeover: life insurer (CI/IP/LTCI), composite insurer, PMI company, CI portfolio from a life insurer, PMI portfolio from a composite/short-term insurer. First three are company takeovers; latter two are portfolio takeovers.
Main task: place a value on the target company or portfolio.

Value of proprietary company for takeover = embedded value + goodwill = full appraisal value. Embedded value = PV of future profits from existing business + net assets attributable to shareholders. Goodwill = value to shareholders of expected future profits from future new business.
Calculations should use realistic assumptions; sensitivity analysis essential.

Key questions before proceeding: purpose of takeover? Hostile or willing? Viable to do preparatory work? Method/data/accuracy/timeframe/margins? Legal/commercial restrictions? Risks? Responsibilities on completion?
Failure to understand what is being taken on can result in financial disaster. Factors to consider are covered in Sections 3.1–3.12.
key_terms: full appraisal value: embedded value + goodwill (value of expected future new business profits)
embedded value: PV of future profits from existing business + net assets attributable to shareholders

exam_qa.q: How would the value of a proprietary company be calculated for takeover purposes?
exam_qa.a: Full appraisal value = embedded value + goodwill. Embedded value = PV of future profit stream from existing business + value of net assets separately attributable to shareholders. Goodwill = value to shareholders of future profits expected from future new business. Calculations should be on realistic assumptions with sensitivity analysis.

subsections.title: Willing Buyer and/or Willing Seller
bullets: Research needed into willingness of buyer to purchase and reasons seller wishes to dispose (e.g. seller may be unable to manage the operation effectively).
Common reasons for purchase/sale: need for more capital; need for more distribution channels; to reduce expenses (economies of scale or synergies).
Also consider whether sale/purchase is the best method — alternatives include reinsurance, mergers, expense efficiencies.

subsections.title: Data Availability and Quality
bullets: Far more data must be collected on health insurance than for life insurance — many companies fail to understand this.
Data quality always an issue in health (for both insurers and medical providers).

Summary data may be available; can source data be accessed? ('due diligence'). Willingness of seller affects data access: willing seller provides detailed data; hostile takeover target does not (purchaser limited to publicly available information).

Advantage to willing seller of making data available: purchaser can offer a more accurate (generally higher) price, and has better understanding of the business → better chance it will be run well, which suits existing management and staff.

Why health data requires more collection: (i) products more complex (many claim reasons, options, rating factors); (ii) sickness experience more uncertain and volatile than mortality; (iii) claims data more complex (recoveries under IP/LTCI, multiple disability levels under LTCI, cost variation under PMI); (iv) external data less widely available (competitive reasons or product immaturity); (v) data more likely to be less relevant due to greater product variation in health.

exam_qa.q: Why does 'far more data' have to be collected on health products than for life business?
exam_qa.a: Health products are more complex (many reasons for claiming, more options, more rating factors e.g. occupation for IP); sickness experience is more uncertain and volatile than mortality; claims data more complex (recoveries under IP/LTCI, multiple disability levels, cost variation under PMI); external data less widely available; data more likely to be less relevant to business currently written due to greater product variation.

subsections.title: Models
bullets: Possible to model a portfolio or entire insurer to determine appraisal value; time available in a takeover may be very limited.
H&C insurance subject to material variability; difficult to predict long-term — models and projections must be treated with due caution.
Sensitivity testing of key assumptions is particularly important; keep model limitations firmly in mind.
Existing models of the target should be carefully reviewed.

subsections.title: Precedents in Market
bullets: Research past similar sales: identify circumstances, results achieved, and lessons learned.
Helps calibrate approach and avoid repeating historical mistakes.

subsections.title: Regulatory Issues
bullets: May be regulatory or legal requirements before insurance business can transfer (e.g. court or regulator approval required).
Cross-reference: Chapter 10 for more detail.

subsections.title: Adequacy of Reserves
bullets: Purchasing company's actuary must determine whether current reserves are adequate; if not, what measures are needed — can the purchaser inject necessary capital?
Risk: if portfolio transfers to a smaller operation, reserve increases may be required.
Ideally, model the purchaser's business before and after takeover to assess impact; data quality is a key issue.

subsections.title: Certain Liabilities Ring-Fenced
bullets: Where some liabilities are too uncertain to value, they may not be transferred to the purchaser.
Ring-fencing options: (i) specific liabilities reinsured; (ii) specific liabilities retained by the seller; (iii) guarantee given by seller to cap claims outgo.
In general, agreement is usually reached on global reserve figures encompassing purchaser's reasonable estimates.

subsections.title: Synergies
bullets: H&C products are all different — synergies only arise after significant hard work; treat all synergy claims and forecasts with caution.

Potential synergies: cross-selling to different policyholder databases; pockets of expertise in one company absent in the other; reduced overhead costs through integrated operations; increased purchasing power for medical treatments or LTCI.
Cross-selling requires identifying which policyholders of Product X would buy Product Y — may require significant system development; prospective customers may not be as receptive as expected.
Each potential synergy must be thought through carefully.

subsections.title: Market Timing
bullets: Market timing only matters where purchaser and/or seller is a quoted company; compliance with Stock Exchange rules required.

General Stock Exchange principles: (i) reasonable offers made for shares (at least current share price); (ii) management treats all potential buyers fairly and equally; (iii) standard timetable established and adhered to (announcement to all investors at same time); (iv) reasonable conditions attached to sale. Purpose: ensure all shareholders treated fairly and maintain market confidence.

subsections.title: Shareholder Approval
bullets: If shareholder approval required, must consider cost of obtaining it and delay to completion.
Actuary may be involved in producing or approving shareholder literature to ensure it contains a true and fair view of the issues.

subsections.title: Ability to Service Claims and Administration
bullets: Acquiring company must be able to service claims and administer the business on purchase — either in-house or outsourced.
Benefits of purchase can be immediately lost through failure to service business adequately: claims outstanding increase, losses to company follow.

subsections.title: Monopoly Considerations
bullets: Regulatory agencies may become interested when an insurer acquires a significant market share in any branch of insurance.
Prior discussions with regulatory agencies may be required at an early stage of takeover discussions.
Risk: proceeding through an expensive takeover process only to find it is disallowed on monopoly grounds — must be checked early.

subsections.title: Introduction and Sources Overview
bullets: Sources of data for health actuarial analyses: (i) own experience; (ii) local (national) population data; (iii) reinsurers' data; (iv) market data on insured lives; (v) supervisory reports; (vi) trade magazines; (vii) actuarial consultants; (viii) overseas data; (ix) rate table software.
These points are relevant to many health actuarial analyses, not just the three strategic assessments in Sections 1–3; similar considerations apply to data used for product pricing (Ch8).

exam_qa.q: List the sources of data that can be used to analyse the claims experience of health insurance products.
exam_qa.a: Own experience; local (national) population data; reinsurers' data; market data on insured lives; supervisory reports; trade magazines; actuarial consultants; overseas data; rate table software.

subsections.title: Own Experience
bullets: Only data set reflecting the company's particular circumstances, distribution channels and product variations.
Not relevant for company-launch or portfolio-launch scenarios; only partially relevant for takeover/merger.
H&C data sets need to be collected over a long period; more data than life equivalent needed for same credibility.

Why PMI own experience loses relevance faster than long-term products: PMI is annually renewable (conditions may change year to year); non-renewal rates greater than lapses on long-term products (customer must actively decide to renew; significant premium increases with medical inflation vs level premiums under long-term products); PMI claims experience also affected by medical inflation (difficult to adjust); long-term health products provide fixed or indexed benefits.
Claims and in-force databases often cannot practically be merged for H&C; claims database must hold extensive data for multiple investigations.
Regular monitoring often on monthly basis; additional risk factors may be identified over time and added to data collection.

Initial claim analysis: by type/level of claim, age (in bands), gender, sales channel. As experience builds, can split further: location, occupation, smoker status, age in more detail, number of people covered, policy duration. Uses: pricing rating factors; understanding mix of business; identifying causes of poor experience and taking corrective action.

exam_qa.q: Why is own experience more of a problem for PMI than for long-term health products in terms of data relevance?
exam_qa.a: PMI is annually renewable so policy conditions may change from year to year, making past experience less relevant for the future. Non-renewal rates are greater than lapses on long-term products (conscious renewal decision; premium increases significantly with medical inflation, whereas long-term products often have level premiums). PMI experience is also affected by medical cost inflation, whereas long-term products provide fixed or indexed benefits. The underlying exposure therefore changes more significantly year-on-year under PMI.

subsections.title: Wider Published Market Experience
bullets: UK: data published for CI and IP — CMI (Continuous Mortality Investigation of the IFoA) produces reports and working papers on group and individual IP experience and accelerated/stand-alone CI. Needs adjustment to reflect specific insurer's experience.
PMI providers in UK fiercely protective of own data (fear of losing competitive edge) → very little published beyond supervisory reports and published accounts.
UK LTCI: very little published data — product relatively new (first UK products 1991); low volumes sold; long-term nature means very few claims have emerged.

subsections.title: Population / Health Statistics
bullets: Drawbacks of population data: (i) national experience not directly relevant to insured lives sub-set (may improve with socio-economic/region/age splits); (ii) disability circumstances unlikely to exactly match policy claim definitions; (iii) accuracy/reliability questionable in healthcare (subjective definitions, double counting); (iv) data may not be in appropriate format or electronically available; (v) may be out-of-date by time of publication.

Insured population generally a sub-set of general population; exception: compulsory universal schemes (e.g. Switzerland — basic health insurance compulsory for all regardless of age, gender, or pre-existing conditions).

Where insurance is not compulsory, insured experience tends to be better than general population for most (not all) H&C products. Population data sets can be used if nothing else is available but may need adjustment.

UK CI population data sources: MSGP (Morbidity Statistics from General Practice — GP consultation reasons by ICD code, all health regions England & Wales); HES (Hospital Episode Statistics — all NHS hospital admissions in England; excludes: no hospital treatment, private treatment, died before admission completion); ONS Cancer Registrations (cancer incidence by type/gender/5-year age bands, England & Wales; incidence only, not other CI conditions); ONS Cancer Survival Trends (mortality after CI; cancer only); Charities (Cancer Research UK, British Heart Foundation — disease incidence and survival data).
key_terms: CMI (Continuous Mortality Investigation): IFoA body that produces UK market experience data on IP and CI
HES (Hospital Episode Statistics): records all NHS hospital admissions in England; excludes private treatment and those not admitted
MSGP (Morbidity Statistics from General Practice): GP consultation data split by ICD code, England & Wales
ICD code: internationally recognised classification system for illnesses

exam_qa.q: What are the drawbacks of using population data for health insurance pricing?
exam_qa.a: National experience may not be relevant to the insured sub-set of lives; disability definitions unlikely to match policy claim definitions; accuracy and reliability may be questionable (subjective healthcare definitions, scope for double counting); data may not be electronically available or in the right format; population experience may be out-of-date before publication.

subsections.title: Supervisory Reports of Key Players or Targets
bullets: Supervisory returns (regulatory reports) are useful but reflect others' experience, not the company's own; give an indication of costing and reserving bases but may need adjustment.
Insurers may show only sample inception and recovery/termination rates rather than declaring full valuation bases — limiting their value.

subsections.title: Reinsurer Information
bullets: Reinsurer data is of reasonable quality but reflects other competitors' or public healthcare experience — adjustment required before use.
Actuary must understand what the data relates to: product details, territories, level of underwriting, expected impact of anti-selection or moral hazard, time period.

Reinsurer is keen to provide accurate and relevant data because: they share in the insurer's experience (extent depends on type and level of reinsurance); inaccurate data leads to unprofitable business; reinsurer also wants the business to continue so that reinsurance coverage (and associated income) continues.

exam_qa.q: Why is the reinsurer keen to ensure that data provided to an insurer is as accurate and relevant as possible?
exam_qa.a: The reinsurer shares in the insurer's experience (to the extent determined by the reinsurance type and level); inaccurate data leads to decisions and premium rates that result in unprofitable business, which affects the reinsurer's own results. The reinsurer also wants the business to continue so that it can maintain its reinsurance coverage.

subsections.title: Data from Consultants and Overseas Market Data
bullets: Consultants may have data sets from the insurer's own country or from elsewhere for specialist use; in some countries consultant data is the major data source.
Such data collected from a different population → other characteristics embedded → adjustments required before use.

UK LTCI: no published UK data; overseas sources available: Long-Term Care Intercompany Experience Study (Society of Actuaries); Association of Health Insurance Companies (AHIC) data from the German compulsory private insurance market.
When using overseas data for other territories: must allow for differences in insured population, product design, underwriting, claims control, etc.

21.summary:
Assessment of the market for the launch of a new company
• Consider: whether the products meet consumer needs and are affordable; the ability of the company to create consumer confidence in the product offered; the potential distribution channels; the level of competition in the particular market.
• Other factors that should be considered in evaluating a launch include: the profitability of existing players in the market; the availability and relevance of data; the need for new staff and systems; the availability of capital.

Assessment of a company or portfolio for takeover
• Factors to consider include: the willingness of the buyer and / or seller, and the reason for sale; the availability and quality of data; how to value the company – often an appraisal value is calculated based on ‘reasonable’ assumptions, using a discounted cashflow model; results should be viewed with caution and sensitivity testing carried out; precedents in the market; regulatory issues; adequacy of reserves (before and after takeover); certain liabilities that may be ring-fenced in various ways; the ‘benefits’ of synergies – view with caution; market timing issues, if the company is quoted; implications of obtaining shareholder approval, if required; the ability of the acquiring company to service claims and administer business; monopoly considerations.

Assessment of overseas markets
• Important to make local contacts to provide knowledge, assistance and help understand the influences in the overseas market, including: the level and type of competition; the provision of healthcare by the State; the distribution channels; the impact of local culture and religion; legal and regulatory matters.
• Reinsurers can help make contacts.
• Microinsurance is one way to expand into less developed overseas markets.

Finding appropriate data / information for the above analyses
• Data may be obtained from: insurer’s own experience; published insurance market experience; population / health statistics; supervisory reports; reinsurers; consultants; overseas data.
• Issues of quality, credibility and relevance need to be considered.
• All data should be in a consistent format.
• Data should be monitored regularly and analyses enhanced as experience builds up.

22.title: Recommending Strategies: Full Synthesis

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter addresses scenarios where the actuary must recommend strategies for future action — as distinct from merely assessing a situation (Ch21).
Recommendations typically arise from a one-off analysis or ongoing experience monitoring; the emphasis is on developing concrete action plans based on experience analysis.
The key advice is to understand the principles underlying each strategy rather than rote-learning specific details, as exam questions may cover scenarios not explicitly in the reading.

subsections.title: When and Why
bullets: New product development is required when: the existing market is saturated, or new customer acquisition rates are economically unviable.
New products may also be needed to maintain overall profitability in a changing market — a fundamental aspect of long-term insurer strategy.
Product design and pricing considerations are covered in Chapters 7 and 8.

subsections.title: Sources of New Product Ideas
bullets: Investigation of the health and care insurance marketplace.
Reinsurers — especially useful for ideas not yet in the domestic market.
Internal innovation (e.g. the UK 'NHS six-week rule' PMI concept, developed in the 1980s by a major PMI provider and subsequently widely adopted).
Market research and external sources.

subsections.title: Reasons for Abandonment
bullets: Insufficient profitability or losses; too risky; insufficient sales volumes or persistency; too capital intensive; does not fit brand image; desire to reduce administrative complexity.

Underlying causes include: deteriorating claims experience (anti-selection, trends, inflation, poor claims control); inadequate pricing (e.g. due to inadequate data); increased competition; changes in environment, state provision, economy, or medical science; overly onerous guarantees; changes in overall insurer strategy.

subsections.title: Mechanics of Abandonment
bullets: Long-term business: abandonment means stopping new sales; the insurer remains legally obliged to continue cover on existing policies until contract expiry.
