• The available capital for a mutual typically from: initial (and very occasionally subsequent) capital injections; past under-distribution of surplus in respect of with-profits policies.
• Realistic capital is the best measure of available capital from a policyholder and shareholder perspective.

Assessing ongoing solvency
• Projections of the ongoing solvency position are used to assess future capital requirements.
• Economic capital – Realistic capital can be determined using a number of scenarios, using stochastic modelling or stress tests. The time horizon considered could be a one-year period or the full run-off of the in-force business.
• Regulatory capital – Most likely risk sensitive and targeting a different measure of policyholder security than any internal measure. However, any regulatory requirement to undertake an ORSA may include a solvency capital projection which may be equivalent (or similar) to an internal economic capital assessment.
• Rating agency capital – Should understand the capital adequacy standards used by rating agencies as this can drive the cost of raising additional capital.
• Solvency projections can be complicated by the need for nested stochastic simulations, simplifications may be used, eg closed-form solutions and proxy models.

The relationship between capital, risk and economic value
• Risk, capital and value are inextricably linked: risk exposure drives capital needs; the market price of financial risk drives the shareholders' required return; shareholder value is modified by the frictional costs of holding capital.

Methods of managing the capital position
• Methods that can raise additional available capital or reduce liabilities and/or required capital include: equity; subordinated loan stock; securitisation; reinsurance; derivatives – both static and dynamic strategies; asset-liability matching.
• Securitisation and financial reinsurance are less effective in regulatory regimes which take credit for future profits in the regulatory balance sheet, eg under Solvency II.

17.title: Uses and Benefits of Reinsurance

subsections.title: Chapter Overview
bullets: This chapter builds on SP1 coverage of reinsurance types and reasons, going deeper into why a health and care insurer would use reinsurance.
The main reasons covered are: (1) control of risks and smoothing profits; (2) financial assistance; (3) technical assistance.
The final sections examine the impact of reinsurance on an insurer's profitability, and the use of 'badging' as an alternative to reinsurance.

subsections.title: Reinsurance Types Overview
bullets: Facultative: each risk is considered and reinsured separately.
Treaty: a contract between insurer and reinsurer that enables many risks to be included under one reinsurance arrangement.

Proportional: an agreed proportion of each risk is ceded. Two forms — quota share (same proportion of all risks ceded) and surplus (retention is fixed, proportion varies by risk; not applicable to short-term health insurance). Basis can be original terms (coinsurance; reinsurer takes a proportion of the office premium) or risk premium (a specific reinsurance premium is charged for the risk reinsured).

Non-proportional: capping of claims. Normally only used in short-term or group health insurance. Forms include excess of loss (operating on each risk, on an aggregation of claims, or on a catastrophe basis) and stop loss (covers loss ratios between limits for a whole account over a certain period).
Financial reinsurance: used to assist the financial position of the cedant, e.g. as part of risk premium reinsurance.

key_terms.definition: Each risk is individually assessed and reinsured separately between insurer and reinsurer.
A standing contract where many risks are automatically included under a single reinsurance arrangement.
Proportional reinsurance where the same fixed proportion of all risks in a portfolio is ceded to the reinsurer.
Proportional reinsurance where the cedant's retention is fixed per risk, and the proportion ceded varies; not applicable to short-term health insurance.
Non-proportional reinsurance that caps individual claim amounts, aggregate claims, or catastrophe events above a defined retention.
Non-proportional reinsurance covering loss ratios between defined limits for a whole account over a period.
Reinsurance used primarily to improve the cedant's financial position rather than to transfer underwriting risk.

exam_qa.q: List the types of reinsurance used by health and care insurers.
exam_qa.a: Facultative; Treaty; Proportional (quota share or surplus; on original terms or risk premium basis); Non-proportional (excess of loss — per risk, aggregate or catastrophe — or stop loss); Financial reinsurance.

exam_qa.q: Why is surplus reinsurance not applicable to short-term health insurance?
exam_qa.a: Surplus reinsurance fixes a retention per risk and varies the ceded proportion — this suits long-term risks with large and varying sums at risk. Short-term health insurance products typically do not generate the large or varying individual sums at risk that make surplus reinsurance practical.

subsections.title: Limitation of Exposure to Risk
bullets: Despite statistical analysis and experienced underwriters, claims outgo from any period cannot be predicted with accuracy — this uncertainty is the essence of insurance.

The insurer's appetite for using reinsurance to offset risk is determined by its size, marketplace experience, available free assets, portfolio size (credibility factor), and confidence in the predictability of business outcomes.

Where an insurer fears that a loss or combination of losses could materially (negatively) affect results within a business line, reinsurance will be considered — whether arising from a single event, related events across portfolios, exposure to a particular peril, or geographic concentration.
Using reinsurance mitigates the need for large margins in mortality (and other) assumptions, which can allow more competitive premium rates.

exam_qa.q: What factors determine an insurer's appetite for using reinsurance to offset risk?
exam_qa.a: The insurer's size; experience in the marketplace; available free assets; size of portfolio (credibility factor); and the degree to which the business outcome is felt to be predictable within bounds.

subsections.title: Large Risks and Smoothing of Profit
bullets: What constitutes a 'large risk' depends heavily on the insurer's size and, in particular, the extent of its free reserves.

Long-term products: large individual risks can arise on critical illness and income protection insurance. Large sums insured can also arise on specialist long-term care insurance products for younger policyholders (pre-funded LTCI builds up large funds to meet LTC costs). The direct insurer can reinsure an element of this risk on a facultative or treaty basis to stabilise emerging profits.
A very high sum insured on an IP policy (e.g. for a key employee) may be reinsured facultatively because of the individual nature of the risk or because it falls outside standard treaty conditions.

Where an insurer lacks sufficient capital backing, it may be reluctant or unable to accept particular risks by sector or volume. Reinsurance cover can assist — solvency requirements for a line of business are normally reduced in line with the proportion ceded (subject to an upper limit). Surplus treaty reinsurance is particularly relevant here.

Short-term products: large payouts can arise on private medical expenses contracts where the insured requires an expensive procedure or large amount of ongoing treatment (e.g. cancer). These amounts are reinsured using non-proportional methods (Section 2.6).

exam_qa.q: How does reinsurance assist an insurer with insufficient capital that is reluctant to accept large risks?
exam_qa.a: Reinsurance cover (e.g. surplus treaty) reduces solvency requirements for that line of business in line with the proportion ceded (subject to an upper limit), allowing the insurer to write business it could not otherwise accept.

subsections.title: Technical Assistance (within Risk Control)
bullets: A reinsurer can also provide technical assistance and control in the areas of underwriting, claims approval and claims monitoring.
This is covered in more detail in Section 4.

subsections.title: Group Schemes
bullets: A large portfolio of group schemes does not present a problem to a direct insurer provided necessary reinsurance is taken to cover large sums at risk on individuals within the group — using a surplus basis.

Group schemes may be more prone to accumulation of risk, requiring aggregate or catastrophe excess of loss reinsurance. Examples of triggering events: outbreak of infectious disease or workplace explosion (giving rise to multiple IP or CI claims); legal risk leading to payment of many CI claims; or introduction of a group-wide screening programme generating more claims.
For group medical insurance, reinsurance is useful on a proportional basis to improve capacity.

exam_qa.q: Why might group schemes require aggregate or catastrophe excess of loss reinsurance?
exam_qa.a: Group schemes are more prone to accumulation of risk — for example, an infectious disease outbreak or workplace explosion could generate many simultaneous IP or CI claims; a legal risk or group screening programme could trigger many CI claims. Standard per-risk covers would not protect against such correlated events.

subsections.title: Concentration of Risks
bullets: An insurer must reinsure where there are a small number of large group schemes or a group scheme presenting a large concentration of risk (possibly by occupation or geography). Similar concentrations can arise by area, industry, or product.
Such schemes may be reinsured on a catastrophe basis or by quota share.
A small number of group schemes will not be diversified and so could be prone to concentration of risk.

subsections.title: Control of Portfolio Risk
bullets: Diversification: an accumulation of health insurance risks can present an unwelcome concentration of liabilities — the insurer will be keen to diversify the spread of risk to control the effect on the overall portfolio.

Overall account management: reinsurance covering larger risks or accumulations above certain limits helps achieve smooth year-on-year account development, especially when the portfolio is relatively immature. Stop loss reinsurance is used for this purpose, though it may not be available at an affordable price.

New products and new risk areas: when an insurer moves into new risk areas with little previous experience, the reinsurer can help with product development, rating, underwriting and claims management. Coverage method is typically quota share with a low initial retention, reducing as the insurer's expertise grows.

Avoiding concentration by product: where a portfolio is heavily imbalanced due to distribution strategy, pricing strategy, or limited product range, reinsurance can relieve this concentration. The reinsurer reduces the cedant's exposure to a particular risk and may organise retrocession to diversify risk across the portfolio. The reinsurer may also assist with launching new products to aid diversification.
Retrocession is reinsurance of reinsurance — it cedes a reinsurer's risk in the same way that reinsurance cedes the insurer's risk.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance of reinsurance — the reinsurer cedes part of its own risk to another reinsurer, analogous to how a direct insurer cedes risk to the reinsurer.
Non-proportional cover that limits the cedant's loss ratio on a whole account to between defined limits over a period, used to smooth overall account results.

exam_qa.q: What is retrocession and why might a reinsurer use it?
exam_qa.a: Retrocession is reinsurance of reinsurance — it cedes a reinsurer's risk in the same way that reinsurance cedes an insurer's risk. A reinsurer may use retrocession to diversify the spread of risks across its own portfolio, particularly when helping a cedant avoid concentration by product.

subsections.title: Financing of New Business Strain
bullets: An insurer can obtain financial assistance via reinsurance arrangements — all fundamentally involve an initial transfer of funds from reinsurer to insurer.

One major use is to enable new business to be written. Under proportional reinsurance, solvency requirements are normally reduced in line with the proportion ceded (subject to an upper limit). Quota share is the normal method.

Where a distribution strategy involves substantially more cash outflow than premium income in the initial stages, reinsurance commission may be available to 'factor' future surplus streams — i.e. lend now against predicted future flows of premiums less expenses and claims. In these circumstances the reinsurer may be taking the lapse risk in whole or in part.

Many reinsurers will be unwilling to take on lapse risk. If the policy lapses, the insurer loses the opportunity to recoup early expenditure and cannot repay the reinsurer for the financial assistance.

What happens if the policy lapses is specified in treaty conditions, usually in the form of a commission clawback arrangement. Some early strains (e.g. administration and underwriting costs) may not be recouped. The risk taken on by either party is factored into the whole reinsurance arrangement (e.g. by changing commission terms).

key_terms.definition: The initial negative cash flow arising when a new policy is written — set-up costs and reserve establishment exceed the first premium received. Financial reinsurance can fund this strain.
A treaty provision requiring the ceding insurer to repay reinsurance commission if a policy lapses before the reinsurer has recouped its initial financial assistance.
The risk that policyholders cancel policies before the insurer (or reinsurer) can recoup early expenditure. Under financial reinsurance the reinsurer may take this risk wholly or in part.

exam_qa.q: How can reinsurance assist with new business strain?
exam_qa.a: Under proportional reinsurance, solvency requirements are reduced in line with the proportion ceded. Additionally, reinsurance commission can 'factor' predicted future surplus streams — effectively lending funds against future premiums less expenses and claims — thereby funding the initial new business strain. The reinsurer takes on the lapse risk in whole or in part.

subsections.title: Solvency
bullets: Financial reinsurance has historically been used to improve the regulatory balance sheet by crystallising the value of future expected profits. However, the effectiveness of such arrangements is much reduced (or eliminated) under Solvency II, since any funding provided by the reinsurer is offset by the need to repay this amount in the future.

It may be possible to construct an arrangement allowing credit in the Solvency II balance sheet for future profits not otherwise allowed (e.g. those arising beyond the contract boundary). The realistic balance sheet will not include profits beyond the contract boundary — so if the insurer 'sells' these profits to the reinsurer, its assets increase without any change in regulatory liabilities.
Depending on local regulation, financial reinsurance may still be effective in jurisdictions where Solvency II or a similar regime does not apply.

Traditional reinsurance can reduce risk-based capital requirements such as the Solvency II SCR: as risk is transferred to the reinsurer, the morbidity risk is reduced (though counterparty default risk increases). This should reduce both the SCR and the risk margin, and also reduce the volatility of the realistic balance sheet.

key_terms.definition: Under Solvency II, the point beyond which the insurer has no obligation to continue providing insurance and the policyholder has no obligation to pay premiums. Profits beyond this boundary cannot be included in the realistic balance sheet.

exam_qa.q: Why is the effectiveness of financial reinsurance much reduced under Solvency II?
exam_qa.a: Under Solvency II the realistic balance sheet will be little changed by financial reinsurance, because any funding provided by the reinsurer must be offset by the liability to repay it in the future. The net effect on available capital is therefore approximately zero.

exam_qa.q: How can traditional reinsurance reduce a company's Solvency II SCR?
exam_qa.a: As risk is transferred to the reinsurer, the morbidity risk in the insurer's portfolio is reduced (although the counterparty default risk increases). This should reduce both the SCR and the risk margin, and also reduce the volatility of the realistic balance sheet.

subsections.title: Financing of Takeovers
bullets: Financial assistance from reinsurers is not only useful for writing new business; it can also facilitate acquisition of an insurance company.

Where a subset (or all) of policies in the company being acquired is identified as potentially profitable in the future, the reinsurer is prepared to advance funds in anticipation of this future profit. The reinsurer pays an initial commission; in return, the reinsurer is entitled to a proportion of the future surpluses arising on this portfolio for as long as the arrangement is in place. The reinsurer again takes the lapse risk.
This arrangement is essentially the same as financing new business strain — just applied to a takeover context.

subsections.title: Securitisation
bullets: It is also possible to securitise elements of a health and care insurance portfolio. Potential investors are likely to have specialist knowledge of this area.
Securitisation takes an insurance risk and develops an arrangement that looks more like a financial deal, taking the risk one step towards the investment market.
The effectiveness of securitisation is also much reduced under Solvency II, as this approach also involves the selling on of future profits.

key_terms.definition: The conversion of insurance cash flows or risks into tradeable financial instruments, allowing capital market investors to assume insurance risk in exchange for expected returns.

subsections.title: Product Development and Data Provision
bullets: Health and care insurance is a specialist area — being able to call on a specialist reinsurer's services can assist the direct insurer across multiple areas: product development and data provision; risk management; overseas developments; and IT software.

New products, markets and techniques: the reinsurer will have established expertise in new products, new markets and the techniques needed to manage this business. Access to this expertise is invaluable in boosting competence within the direct insurer and establishing a sound platform for the business.

Access to data: data availability and quality is an ongoing problem in health and care insurance. Reinsurer data are likely to be of reasonable quality (reflecting experience of other cedants and public healthcare), though all such data sets may need adjustment before use (differences in mix of business, underwriting level, product terms, claim conditions, geography, credibility, time period, policy sizes, target market, sales channels, reinsurance arrangements, inflation, mortality/morbidity trends, currency, etc.).

Reinsurers have access to a larger volume of data since they cover many cedants and territories. Although a reinsurer would not release data from an individual cedant, high-level aggregate results on a 'no-names basis' might be available.

Sharing of risk: any entry into a new product market has potential pitfalls. The ability of a reinsurer to share some of the risk enables the direct insurer to control profit exposure, and the resulting alignment of objectives helps to ensure quality of the advice and technical assistance provided.

Training: entry into a new health and care product market requires trained staff. This is far more important than in life insurance because elements of the health and care business impact quickly on the direct insurer. Reinsurers will generally offer staff training — including training on credibility in pricing, assumed IBNR and expense bases. Where the reinsurer has a large say in administration (underwriting, pricing), it is more likely to act as reinsurer on favourable terms.

Assessment of future trends: a reinsurer may be in a better position to form an educated opinion on future cost and incidence trends, through research and data aggregation the direct insurer might be unable to carry out. This will allow the direct insurer to underwrite risks that might otherwise be unacceptable (especially for long-term guarantees).

exam_qa.q: List the factors you would take into account when considering adjustments to reinsurer data before use.
exam_qa.a: Mix of business (product type, customer mix); level of underwriting; product terms and conditions (including options and other features); claim conditions and control; geography; credibility and time period; effects of medical advances; policy sizes; target market; sales channel or processes; reinsurance arrangements; competitiveness; timing of payments; effects of inflation on sums insured; trends in mortality and morbidity; currency.

subsections.title: Risk Management
bullets: Underwriting: reinsurers can provide a range of medical and financial underwriting services to assist in the development and continuing control and administration of products. In some jurisdictions, use of moratoria, automatic acceptance and blanket exclusions for PMI and health cash products may reduce the need for underwriting training. By contrast, underwriting training is important for IP and CI products — a mistake can cost both insurer and reinsurer a great deal of money on an early claim. Services include training assistance and the provision of an underwriting manual.

An underwriting manual would typically include: rules on acceptance at standard rates without further medical evidence; sums insured limits requiring further medical evidence (supplementary questionnaires, blood tests, medical examinations); information required at each underwriting stage; exclusions; minimum and maximum premiums and benefits; acceptance of unusual risks (loadings for strange occupations, pastimes, medical conditions); referrals to senior underwriting staff and medical practitioners; and a detailed medical section.

Increasingly, reinsurers will not accept underwriting risk and will audit a cedant's underwriting practice instead. Whether the reinsurer refuses to honour a claim after a cedant underwriting mistake depends on factors such as the business relationship, frequency of occurrence, and the wording of the reinsurance treaty.

Claims control: an essential component for establishing and maintaining profitability. Reinsurers can assist in claims management (e.g. initial assessment), training in claims handling, and monitoring of claims experience.

Health insurance claims control is more critical than in life or general insurance: policyholders may make false declarations on application; claims can be made multiple times during the contract period; some claims have dubious validity; policy conditions can be complex; and for long-term insurance the insurer is tied to the policy at fixed premium rates for many years.

Policy wording and product design: shortfalls or defects in policy wording can result in claims that were not intended to be within scope. Regular reviews of claims experience are needed to identify such defects and incorporate solutions into the next policy draft. Problems can arise in the policy document, application forms, and marketing literature. Reinsurers can assist with such reviews.

key_terms.definition: A set of rules and guidelines provided (often by the reinsurer) covering acceptance at standard rates, evidence requirements, exclusions, premium/benefit limits, unusual risk loadings, referral procedures, and a medical reference section.

The process of initially assessing, approving, monitoring and managing insurance claims. Particularly critical in health insurance where claims can recur, conditions are complex, and moral hazard is higher than in life insurance.

exam_qa.q: Why is claims control more important for a health insurer than for a life or general insurer?
exam_qa.a: In life insurance, the claim event (death) is a one-off and easily verified. In general insurance, short-term policy nature allows premium adjustment at renewal. In health insurance: policyholders may make false declarations on application; multiple claims can be made during the contract period; some claims have dubious validity and are difficult to check; policy conditions can be complex; and for long-term products the insurer is tied to the policy at fixed premium rates for many years. Therefore claims control is vital.

exam_qa.q: What would you find in an underwriting manual provided by a reinsurer?
exam_qa.a: Rules on acceptance at standard rates without further medical evidence; sums insured limits requiring further evidence (supplementary questionnaires, blood tests, medical examinations); information required at each underwriting stage; exclusions; minimum and maximum premiums and benefits; acceptance of unusual risks including loading calculations; referrals to senior staff and medical practitioners; and a detailed medical section to assist underwriting decisions.

subsections.title: Overseas Developments
bullets: Local risks: all technical assistance a reinsurer can provide for a domestic insurer is even more important when looking at products sold outside the insurer's home market. Understanding the interplay locally between State provision and private provision is crucial. Reinsurers can provide copies of local policy documents and underwriting and claim forms, with translations where necessary.

Local establishment and contacts: local knowledge is required to help an insurer get established in an overseas territory, as well as key contacts — distributors; third-party administration companies; consultants performing surgical procedures; hospitals; and others providing ancillary functions (e.g. obtaining medical information at the proposal or claims acceptance stage). Reinsurers can help with all these.

Local data: the actuary will want local risk statistics on claims incidence and, where appropriate, claims amount — to determine whether risk premiums can be derived by adjusting those of another territory, or need building from scratch, and whether premium rates can be written profitably. Reinsurers operating in that territory for some years may be able to provide experience analysis results (adjusted for relevant differences).

Legal and regulatory advice: advice is required on local legal and regulatory matters where local custom and practice must be taken into account. Reinsurers may provide a first-hand guide to local legislation, but professional legal assistance from country-specific experts will always be necessary.

subsections.title: IT Software
bullets: Many reinsurers have produced IT software that distils expert knowledge so that proposal processing can be handled automatically by relatively junior staff. Limits are set such that cases above a certain size or with particular complex features require senior staff attention — but as many as 80% of cases may be accepted by the system in a competent and consistent manner (though for many health insurers this can reduce to 50% or less, depending on the IT system's capability).
Reinsurers may also provide software to assist in claims acceptance — the system can access the original proposal form information and check for inconsistencies with the claim document.

Systems are also available to help with creation of reserves: for example, recording a 'standard' amount to the case estimate of a PMI claim based on its key features, to be updated as more data becomes available.

The sophistication of software packages used to handle pricing, underwriting and claims is growing daily. Health and care insurance pricing is more complex than life insurance as it needs to incorporate multi-state models.

Modern packages rely increasingly on the internet — the insurer can access the latest version on-line, and the reinsurer may assist on a remote but interactive basis. However, there may be problems with the confidentiality of information as it moves between sites.

All health and care insurance products change over time as the health market changes — legislation, regulation, and technological advances all require software to be easily modifiable. Maintaining the package on an internet site enables all users to access the most up-to-date version instantly.

subsections.title: Tax Arbitrage
bullets: Theoretically, reinsurance should be a self-balancing item — the financial net gain is zero: what the insurer pays out in the annual statement (reinsurance premiums less commissions less claims) is what the reinsurer receives, positive or negative. In practice this is not always the case.

If the basis of taxation differs for the reinsurer and the ceding insurer, it may be possible to improve the after-tax bottom line for both companies in certain circumstances by establishing a treaty.

By having extensive reinsurance (particularly in tax-efficient territories), the insurer can use differing tax positions to its advantage, either benefiting directly or passing the benefit on to customers in the form of lower premiums.
The potential for tax arbitrage could make quota share popular, although there is a danger that rules might change in the future.

key_terms.definition: Exploiting differences in the tax treatment of the insurer and reinsurer to improve the after-tax position of both parties through a reinsurance arrangement.

subsections.title: Regulatory Arbitrage
bullets: Solvency capital requirements may be different for reinsurers and direct insurers, resulting in differences in capital cost. This is also known as 'capital arbitrage'.

When the reinsurer does not need to hold as much capital as the insurer for a given unit of risk (e.g. due to a different regulatory capital requirement), the cost of capital for managing the business will be less in the reinsurer's hands. Part of that saving can be transferred to the insurer by offering attractive (cheap) reinsurance premium rates or higher reinsurance commission.

key_terms.definition: Exploiting differences in the solvency capital requirements of the direct insurer and the reinsurer to reduce the overall cost of capital across the two parties.

exam_qa.q: What is capital arbitrage in the context of reinsurance?
exam_qa.a: Capital arbitrage occurs when the reinsurer is required to hold less capital than the insurer for a given unit of risk under their respective regulatory regimes. The lower cost of capital in the reinsurer's hands can be shared with the insurer via cheaper reinsurance premiums or higher commission rates.

subsections.title: New Markets
bullets: When a reinsurer helps an insurer enter a new line of health and care insurance, both companies derive direct benefit: the insurer gains experience and business in-force in an area it might not have entered alone; the reinsurer gains a share (often a quota share) of a potentially profitable tranche of insurance.

The reinsurer can also expand its own experience of that line of business, enhance its credibility as a reinsurance provider in that area, increase its diversification, and influence the risk profile of the book by providing underwriting guidelines.

subsections.title: Consultancy Fees vs Risk Share
bullets: Reinsurers are in business to make money in the long term. There are two ways to do this (not mutually exclusive): by taking a share of expected profitable business (the reinsurer is always seeking a risk share on profitable business); and by charging a fee for services (consultancy fees are more justified where the amount of work undertaken by the reinsurer is excessive relative to the risk share being taken).

In the circumstance of a new product (Section 5.3), the insurer often does not know how successful the launch will be in terms of volume or profit, and hence is often happier if the reinsurer takes a share of the risk rather than charging fees. The partnership with the reinsurer also assists in managing the new line.
In some markets, fees to the reinsurer are rare.

exam_qa.q: In what circumstances would a reinsurer charge consultancy fees rather than taking a risk share?
exam_qa.a: Consultancy fees are more justified where the amount of work undertaken by the reinsurer is excessive relative to the risk share being taken. For new products, a risk share is often preferred by the insurer (and reinsurer) as neither party knows how successful the launch will be — aligning incentives. In some markets, fees to the reinsurer are rare.

subsections.title: Predictability of Results
bullets: Health and care insurance results are less predictable than life insurance results. The use of reinsurance should smooth overall results for the direct insurer.

Although the pure financial gain may favour the reinsurer in certain circumstances, the 'peace of mind' benefit to the smaller insurer faced with a portfolio of potentially volatile health and care insurance contracts is worth the payment.
This section reinforces the argument in Section 2 (reducing risk): for health and care insurance in particular, volatility of results is a concern, and reinsurance addresses this.

subsections.title: Difference of Interests
bullets: There is a difference of interests between insurer and reinsurer. For example, where the direct insurer has ceded a high quota share to the reinsurer: in the event of a claim, the insurer will want to pay with minimum fuss; the reinsurer will require very careful consideration of the claim.
The insurer tends to be more interested in quantity of business; the reinsurer tends to be more interested in quality of business.
The insurer–reinsurer relationship is ongoing — conflicts are not in the best interests of either company (unless large settlements are at stake).

Other objective differences from the treaty itself: one or other party may wish to build volume in a particular health and care class; gain experience in a new class or territory; develop a product for a specific client or distribution channel; or the presence of a reinsurance treaty may be a regulatory requirement for a new company.
When the motives are other than purely financial, the bottom-line result will not reflect the total benefit derived by the two parties.

exam_qa.q: Describe the difference of interests between a direct insurer and its reinsurer where a high quota share has been ceded.
exam_qa.a: The direct insurer, bearing relatively little of each claim, will want to pay claims quickly with minimum fuss. The reinsurer, bearing the majority of each claim, will require very careful consideration of each claim. More broadly, the insurer tends to prioritise quantity of business; the reinsurer prioritises quality of business.

subsections.title: Badging as an Alternative to Reinsurance
bullets: Badging is an arrangement where an insurer provides a policy to the customer under its own brand name, but a specialist health and care insurer actually issues the policy and undertakes all policy processing, claims administration, payments and administration.

Badging reduces insurance risk (and thus the need for additional capital) similarly to reinsurance, but it is not a true alternative to reinsurance in the strict sense — it is a way of accessing someone else's expertise without taking on any insurance risk yourself.

Key distinction: under badging, all risk is taken on by the specialist insurer (at least initially); under reinsurance, the issuing company takes on the risk first, then passes part or all of it to the reinsurer.

Example: Popular Insurance (large, well-known, no health and care experience) can badge with Healthwise (small, specialist). Popular Insurance provides market presence/brand; Healthwise provides health and care expertise. The product carries Popular Insurance's brand name. Both parties benefit — Popular Insurance gains access to health and care expertise; Healthwise gains access to a large customer base. Payment depends on the balance of benefit; a profit-sharing arrangement on a retrospective basis (or regular reviews) is one solution.

Extra risks introduced by badging: poor service by Healthwise could damage Popular Insurance's reputation. Also, pure badging may not be legally permissible — local regulation may require the name of the product provider (Healthwise) to be clearly shown in product literature, so customers know who is actually providing the insurance.

key_terms.definition: An arrangement where a non-specialist insurer sells a product under its own brand, but a specialist insurer issues the policy and handles all administration, claims processing and payments. All insurance risk rests with the specialist insurer.

exam_qa.q: What is badging and how does it differ from reinsurance?
exam_qa.a: Badging is an arrangement where the selling insurer provides a policy to the customer under its own brand, but a specialist health and care insurer issues the policy and undertakes all policy processing, claims administration and payments. Unlike reinsurance, the selling insurer takes on no insurance risk — all risk is borne by the specialist insurer from the outset. Under reinsurance, the issuing company takes on the risk first and then (partly or totally) passes it on to the reinsurer.

exam_qa.q: What are the risks associated with a badging arrangement?
exam_qa.a: If the specialist insurer provides poor service, it could damage the reputation of the branded insurer (whose name is on the policy). Additionally, pure badging may not be legally permissible — regulations may require the name of the actual product provider to be shown clearly in product literature, so the customer knows who is providing the insurance.

17.summary:
Reinsurance is used for the following reasons:
• to control risk: to limit exposure to risk; to enable larger risks to be written; to spread risks
• to smooth profits
• financial assistance: to help with new business strain; to help with solvency position; to help with takeovers
• technical assistance: new products, markets and techniques; access to data; sharing of risk; technical training; assessment of future trends; underwriting; claims control; product design; policy wording; overseas developments; IT software.

Reinsurance should be, but isn't always, a self-balancing item.

Reinsurance can take advantage of tax or regulatory arbitrage.

Reinsurers can either charge insurers a fee for their services and products, or take a share of profitable business, or both.

Badging, where insurers make use of specialist insurers' expertise, is an alternative to reinsurance.

18.title: Asset-Liability Management

subsections.title: Overview and Steps in ALM Modelling
bullets: Investment and asset-liability modelling were introduced in Subject SP1 (Chapter 28) and Chapter 16 (Capital Management).

Steps in using an asset-liability model: (1) project liability outgo in each future time period; (2) project asset proceeds in each future period; (3) compare the two for each future period; (4) re-run comparisons using different assumptions; (5) decide whether asset proceeds are appropriate for the liability outgo; (6) if not, investigate alternative asset distributions.

Chapter structure: Section 1 recaps investment principles; Sections 2–3 cover ALM aims and the effect of liabilities on asset choice; Section 4 outlines the ALM process; Section 5 covers derivatives; Sections 6–9 cover income projections and suitability of specific assets.

exam_qa.q: List briefly the steps involved in using an asset-liability model.
exam_qa.a: Project liability outgo in each future time period; project asset proceeds in each future period; compare the two for each future period; re-run comparisons using different assumptions; decide whether asset proceeds are appropriate for the liability outgo; if not, investigate alternative asset distributions.

subsections.title: The Basic Investment Principle
bullets: For health and care insurance companies, market risk can represent a significant risk exposure, and ALM can be used to help manage required capital.

The basic investment principle: to maximise investment return, subject to meeting all contractual and treating customers fairly (TCF) obligations, and recognising the uncertainties involved and the overall risk that shareholders (of proprietary companies), regulators and policyholders are prepared to tolerate.

The closer a company matches its liabilities with assets of similar terms, amounts, types (including risk exposures) and currencies, the more likely it will be able to meet its contractual liabilities as they fall due.

The extent of any divergence from a matched position depends largely on the degree of risk the company is prepared to take and any disclosure made to policyholders that may force departure from a matched position.

key_terms.definition: A strategy to manage the relationship between assets and liabilities to control market risk and manage required capital.
A regulatory principle requiring insurers to treat customers fairly; a key non-contractual obligation that constrains investment strategy.

subsections.title: Main Aims and Risk Budget
bullets: The main aim of ALM is to derive an investment strategy that takes into account the health and care insurer's attitude to market risk — the extent to which it is prepared to take the risk of losses due to adverse economic and investment market conditions. This varies with the size of available capital and level of diversification.

Available capital is best measured as realistic capital: market value of assets less market-consistent value of liabilities. The solvency cover ratio (e.g. own funds ÷ SCR under Solvency II) is a widely-used metric; insurers generally target 150%–200%.
The insurer must decide the extent to which it is prepared to deviate from cashflow matching — its 'risk budget': how much risk to accept and how much capital to expose in doing so.

Even 'perfect' cashflow matching does not necessarily match the reported liabilities if the liabilities benefit from a volatility adjustment under Solvency II, because the volatility adjustment is based on a hypothetical portfolio rather than the actual assets held.
ALM also aims to examine alternative investment strategies (physical and derivative) so they can be assessed for suitability and level of risk.
A quantitative assessment method must be chosen, e.g. the probability of free capital dropping below zero over the next five years.

Investment strategy is not developed in isolation — it is optimised together with risk management strategy and new business strategy, as part of model office projections feeding into ORSA under Solvency II.
The ALM team also carries out ongoing risk management: measuring, monitoring and rebalancing positions to conform to the agreed strategy.

key_terms.definition: The degree to which an insurer is prepared to deviate from cashflow matching — how much risk to accept and how much capital to expose in doing so.
A Solvency II adjustment applied to the discount rate for liabilities, based on a hypothetical portfolio; movements in the VA are not perfectly replicated by the actual backing assets.
Own Risk and Solvency Assessment — a Solvency II requirement incorporating model office projections and ALM analysis.
Own funds divided by the SCR under Solvency II; typical target range 150%–200%.

sections.title: The Effect of the Liabilities on Asset Selection

subsections.title: Principles
bullets: The insurer must understand and evaluate both contractual and non-contractual liabilities; non-contractual liabilities include TCF obligations and surrender values (e.g. on pre-funded long-term care products).
Asset-liability matching requires not only that there is enough money in total to meet liabilities, but that cashflows are such that money is available at the right time.

subsections.title: Features of Health and Care Insurance Liabilities
bullets: Claim liabilities: high level of uncertainty in timing and amount; occasional large unexpected payments (e.g. major epidemics or high medical costs).
Some liabilities are very short-term (e.g. PMI — settled within a year); most classes stretch to the medium and long term.

In terms of nature, health and care claim liabilities are: (a) guaranteed in monetary terms — uncertainty only in frequency, not amount; (b) guaranteed linked to a prices index — uncertainty in future index value; (c) indemnity (e.g. PMI) — both amount and frequency uncertain, prone to inflationary influences (similar to index-linked); (d) investment-linked — outgo determined directly by underlying investment values.

Treatment of inflation-linked liabilities: for very short-term liabilities (within one year), inflation is unlikely to be a major problem and liabilities are often treated as fixed in monetary terms. For longer-term liabilities, inflation is likely to be a major consideration.
Currency: most liabilities are in the domestic currency; overseas liabilities arise if the insurer underwrites overseas risks or operates overseas branches.
Expense liabilities (servicing existing business, e.g. claim handling): less uncertain than claims; subject to inflation (predominantly wage inflation); mostly in domestic currency.

key_terms.definition: A liability (e.g. PMI) where both the amount and frequency of future claims are uncertain and dependent on costs incurred, which are prone to inflationary influences.

exam_qa.q: Describe the main features of a health and care insurer's claim and expense liabilities.
exam_qa.a: Claim liabilities: high uncertainty in timing and amount; may be short-term (e.g. PMI) or long-term; nature is guaranteed monetary, index-linked, indemnity, or investment-linked; overseas liabilities arise from overseas underwriting. Expense liabilities: less uncertain than claims; subject mainly to wage inflation; mostly in domestic currency.

exam_qa.q: Explain the extent to which income protection, critical illness, PMI and long-term care claims might be linked to a prices or earnings index.
exam_qa.a: IP: could be either index; earnings inflation more likely as indemnity links to salaries. CI (lump sum): usually no index link; if income benefits are paid, escalation rates will be specified in the policy. PMI: escalates mainly with medical costs, which can differ from both RPI and earnings inflation; also affected by new treatments and technology. Long-term care: escalates with care provision costs (mainly salaries) and housing/living costs — a mix of both indices.

subsections.title: Currency
bullets: Liabilities denominated in a particular currency should be matched by assets in the same currency to reduce currency risk.

subsections.title: Free Assets
bullets: Free assets (free reserves or surplus) represent the excess of assets over liabilities.

The size of free assets indicates the extent to which the insurer can depart from normal matching strategies in the hope of improving overall return, thereby benefiting policyholders (through lower premiums) and shareholders (through higher dividends). This is subject to the amount of risk being acceptable.

key_terms.definition: The excess of assets held over liabilities; the larger the free assets, the greater the insurer's freedom to take investment risk and deviate from matched positions.

exam_qa.q: How does the size of the free assets impact the choice of assets for a health and care insurer?
exam_qa.a: Large free assets allow the insurer to depart from strict matching strategies to improve overall return — benefiting policyholders via lower premiums and shareholders via higher dividends — subject to the risk being acceptable.

subsections.title: Regulatory Framework
bullets: Regulators may restrict investment strategy via: types of assets that can be held (prescription and prevention); the amount of any particular asset type admissible for solvency purposes; maximum exposure to a single counterparty/country; extent of currency mismatching (with possible requirement to hold a mismatching reserve); custodianship of assets; investment assumptions used to value liabilities.

exam_qa.q: State the ways in which a regulator may restrict the investment strategy of a health and care insurer.
exam_qa.a: Restrictions on: types of assets (prescription and prevention of holding certain assets); amount of any particular type admissible for solvency; maximum exposure to a single counterparty/country; degree of currency mismatching (and possible mismatching reserve requirement); custodianship of assets; investment assumptions used to value liabilities.

subsections.title: The Mean Term of Assets and Liabilities
bullets: If the mean term of assets is longer than liabilities, a general rise in interest rates will adversely affect financial soundness (asset values fall more than liability values). If assets are shorter, a fall in interest rates will adversely affect solvency.
These statements apply to current solvency only if liabilities are discounted at a market-responsive rate. Future solvency can be threatened in any case.

Assets too long example: liabilities have one-week mean term ($100m), assets have 20-year mean term ($120m). A sudden rise in long-term yields could knock more than 20% off asset values — paper insolvency — though actual insolvency only crystallises if yields are still high when the liability falls due.

Assets too short example: insurer has fixed monetary liability of $100m in two years; holds $85m in cash deposits at 15% p.a.; if interest rates drop to 6%, the cash earnings over two years may be insufficient to meet the liability (compared with two-year bonds which would have locked in the higher rate).
The Solvency II volatility adjustment was designed partly to prevent forced investment strategy changes due to short-term movements in asset values.
The insurer should be aware of the consequences of departing from a matched position by term, but this does not mean the mean terms must necessarily be equal.

exam_qa.q: Under what circumstances might it be acceptable for a health insurer to move away from matching by term?
exam_qa.a: If free assets are extensive, the company can withstand some degree of mismatching. Assets might also be held slightly longer than liabilities if the insurer expects to write new business (using premium income to meet shorter-term liabilities), though this is dangerous if premium income is not guaranteed and should not be used to justify major mismatching.

subsections.title: Inflation
bullets: Inflationary forces are present in: benefits expressly linked to an inflationary index; benefits that indemnify medical expense costs; expenses of settling claims.

Assets selected to support technical reserves may need to increase in value in line with general inflation; national indices (e.g. RPI and CPI in the UK) have some relevance to claims, though they may not perfectly mirror the inflationary forces relevant to specific insurance classes.

Matching the country's inflation-linked index (e.g. RPI in UK): partially achieved by investing in index-linked government bonds, though a time lag in determining payments stops this being a perfect match.
Matching national average earnings (NAE): trickier — bonds linked to NAE are unlikely to exist; equities may provide a suitable long-term match but their short-term volatility can be problematic.
Inflation hedging of business with caps or floors is particularly challenging; inflation-based derivatives may be used as hedging instruments.

key_terms.definition: Government bond whose income and redemption proceeds are linked to an inflation index (e.g. RPI in the UK); useful for matching inflation-linked liabilities though time lags make it imperfect.

subsections.title: Liquidity Requirements
bullets: Classes with widely fluctuating claims levels require access to ready (liquid) funds. Even with reinsurance protection, the insurer must pay gross claims before recovering from reinsurers.
Portfolio contraction means premium income may not cover claims; a growing portfolio may sustain use of incoming premiums to pay claims (though future premium income is not guaranteed).

If claims outgo is highly variable, the investment manager should adopt a cautious strategy with a reasonable proportion of liquid assets. Reinsurance can reduce variability of claims experience, indirectly affecting investment strategy.

Default of a reinsurer or broker will sharply interrupt expected cashflows; if the shortfall is not immediate, assets may be realised in an orderly way to minimise losses from selling at an inappropriate time.

subsections.title: The Effect of Capital Requirements on Asset Selection
bullets: Under Solvency II, an insurer could lose authorisation if capital falls below the Minimum Capital Requirement (MCR); it should therefore avoid all significant risks in assets backing the MCR.

Insurers are expected to plan their finances to hold capital well above the statutory minimum. The safest option for capital backing is short-dated domestic government bonds, offering relative payment security (government guarantee) and low capital value volatility due to imminent maturity.

Insurers hold capital significantly above the statutory minimum: to avoid breaching minimum after unfavourable movements; because authorities expect it; to appear financially strong to brokers, policyholders and shareholders; because statutory minimum may be insufficient given their specific risks.
Solvency II restricts the quality of capital that can be used to cover the insurer's MCR and SCR.

key_terms.definition: Under Solvency II, the minimum level of capital below which the insurer could lose its authorisation to operate.
Under Solvency II, the target level of capital reflecting a 1-in-200-year loss; insurers must hold sufficient own funds to cover this.

exam_qa.q: Why do many insurers aim to hold capital significantly higher than the statutory minimum?
exam_qa.a: To avoid breaching the minimum after unfavourable asset/liability movements; because authorities expect it; to appear financially strong to brokers, policyholders and shareholders (competitive reasons); and because the statutory minimum may be insufficient given their specific risk profile.

subsections.title: Matching by Business Type
bullets: Without-profits business: relatively straightforward to match cashflows, but difficulties arise where suitable assets do not exist or are in limited supply. Long-term liabilities (e.g. deferred annuities, pre-funded long-term care) may be matched using swap instruments in deep and liquid swaps markets (e.g. UK). UK insurers increasingly use 'alternative assets' (infrastructure, equity release mortgages, private credit) to back very long-term business.

Unit-linked business: asset-liability matching is less of an issue as unit liabilities and unit funds are exactly matched. Stochastic investment models are less necessary; sensitivity analysis and scenario testing are used to assess the effect of different scenarios on charges and to plan capital requirements. Actions if profitability is badly affected by asset value falls: warn policyholders of potential charge increases (if consistent with TCF/PRE); sell future charges to a reinsurer; use shareholder funds to purchase derivatives (e.g. put options on an appropriate index); invest unit funds more defensively (though this may conflict with TCF/PRE).

Index-linked business: invest in line with the index or replicate with a smaller selection of stocks. Matching liabilities linked to an economic index (RPI) is partially achieved with index-linked government bonds, though time lags prevent a perfect match. Matching NAE index is trickier — NAE-linked bonds are unlikely to exist; equities may match long-term but have short-term volatility issues.

key_terms.definition: The reasonable expectations of policyholders as to how a product will operate; a constraint on management actions when dealing with unit-linked and with-profits business.
The risk that a derivative hedge (often index-based) does not perfectly offset movements in the actual portfolio of assets held.

subsections.title: ALM Process and Model Types
bullets: Projections of assets and liabilities become particularly useful when the relationship between the two is examined. Either a deterministic or a stochastic model could be used.
Deterministic model: pre-set values for all variables based on a single set of assumptions; calculations may be re-run under different assumptions (sensitivity testing).
Stochastic model: key parameters are randomly selected from a defined probability distribution; enables calculation of probabilities and confidence intervals for outputs.
Stochastic modelling can determine and test management rules for asset-liability matching and enable advance planning for changes in conditions or strategies.

When using a stochastic approach, limitations of models and assumptions must be carefully considered: considerable assumptions are required (asset model choice, calibration); critical assumptions must be identified; the model must be calibrated to current data to produce meaningful results.

The ALM process involves: (a) a liability projection system modelling all relevant cashflows year by year for the policy lifetime; (b) a separate asset projection model (feeding results into the liability system) or combined model. Combining asset and liability models increases the risk of errors and assumption errors.

Allowance for management actions must be carefully considered — if already built into the projection model, the need for matching may be masked, and some actions may be too unpalatable to implement in practice.

key_terms.definition: A model using pre-set values for all variables; multiple runs with different assumptions constitute sensitivity testing.
A model where key parameters are randomly selected from probability distributions, enabling calculation of probabilities and confidence intervals.

exam_qa.q: Explain very briefly the main differences between a deterministic model and a stochastic model.
exam_qa.a: A deterministic model uses pre-set values for all variables based on a single set of assumptions; different assumption sets can be tested (sensitivity testing). A stochastic model treats key parameters as randomly selected from defined probability distributions, enabling probabilities and confidence intervals to be calculated.

subsections.title: Economic Scenario Generators (ESGs)
bullets: Projections under both asset and liability models will be based on the output from an economic scenario generator (ESG): a model that produces numerous simulations of possible future economic outcomes, used as an input to the stochastic projection model.

ESGs typically output macroeconomic variables such as nominal and real interest rates and inflation, and also asset return simulations for equities, commercial property and corporate bonds of different credit ratings.
The power of ESGs comes from their ability to model the joint behaviour of economic variables in a coherent fashion.

ESGs can work over a range of time horizons (months to decades); input parameters are calibrated differently depending on purpose. Common calibrations: 'risk neutral' (or market-consistent) — expected return on all asset types is the risk-free rate, discount rate is also risk-free; 'real world' — model parameters reflect expectations of the future, used to calculate probabilities and confidence intervals.
Market-consistent model: closely reproduces observed prices of quoted assets; provides market-consistent values for unquoted assets or liability cashflows.

Real-world calibration: determines parameters from expectations of the future; calculates probabilities and confidence intervals, e.g. probability that asset values fall below liability values within the next 20 years.
Commercially available ESG models vary in user interfaces, risk drivers and degree of customisation possible.
Time zero stress tests can also be used to assess immediate risks to which the company is exposed; risk exposures may change significantly over time as both liabilities and assets mature.

key_terms.definition: A model that produces numerous simulations of possible future economic outcomes (e.g. interest rates, inflation, asset returns) used as input to stochastic projection models.
A market-consistent calibration where the expected return on all asset types equals the risk-free rate; used for valuation purposes.
An ESG calibration based on expectations of future economic outcomes; used to calculate probabilities and confidence intervals for real-world scenarios.

subsections.title: Allowing for Solvency Requirements
bullets: If the model is required to incorporate assessment of likely solvency at each future period, it must be extended to include estimates of regulatory asset values and liability values, enabling comparison of actual solvency with regulatory requirements.
An insurer will also likely place further, more stringent, class-specific solvency capital requirements on itself.

To consider ongoing progress of the insurer, allowance must be made for future new business. The investigation may assess future solvency under different volumes of new business, different asset distributions, or likely profitability levels.

subsections.title: Use of Derivatives in ALM
bullets: Many health and care insurance firms use derivatives as an integral part of their ALM strategies. They are generally held for the following purposes:

(1) To reduce the risk of losses: as an alternative to physical assets (perhaps when not available), e.g. equity put options to limit loss on an equity market fall; also to gain exposure to physical assets that are difficult or impractical to obtain (e.g. index future rather than buying each individual stock).
(2) To increase available capital or income by reducing regulatory capital requirements: e.g. holding put options may reduce the SCR under Solvency II.

(3) To stabilise the overall balance sheet — e.g. hedging the SCR or risk margin under Solvency II: constructing a portfolio of assets that match any movement in liability values resulting from interest rate changes, reducing balance sheet volatility.
(4) To reduce tax or investment costs: e.g. exchange-traded derivatives are cheaper to deal in than underlying assets.

(5) To more effectively or efficiently acquire or dispose of rights in relation to assets: e.g. going short in equity futures provides an immediate reduction in equity exposure, while allowing actual assets to be sold more slowly as investment conditions become optimal.
Static hedging: derivatives held to protect against a specific severe adverse scenario that only arises in limited circumstances.
Dynamic hedging: a portfolio of derivatives is held and frequently rebalanced to continue matching liability risk sensitivities as conditions change.
Residual risks to consider: counterparty risk, liquidity risk, operational risk, and basis risk (derivative based on an index rather than the actual portfolio held).
Regulatory restrictions on the use of derivatives must also be considered.

key_terms.definition: Holding derivatives to protect against a specific severe adverse scenario that only arises in limited circumstances; not frequently rebalanced.
Holding and frequently rebalancing a portfolio of derivatives to continue matching liability risk sensitivities as market conditions change.
The risk that a derivative hedge (often index-based) does not perfectly offset movements in the actual portfolio of assets held.
A Solvency II component of technical provisions; like best-estimate liabilities, it is sensitive to interest rate changes and may be hedged using derivatives.

sections.title: The Effect of Free Assets on Asset Selection

subsections.title: Free Assets and Investment Freedom
bullets: An insurance company's free assets (or 'free reserves' in a short-term insurer) represent the excess of assets over liabilities.

Some free assets must be held securely to cover minimum solvency capital requirements; the excess may be invested to produce a good long-term return — for shareholders (proprietary) or with-profits policyholders (mutual).

The size of free assets is not measured in absolute terms only; they may be compared with: size of supervisory reserves; premiums written in the last year; ultimate liabilities from a recent year's claims; absolute size of liabilities; regulatory capital requirements.

A proprietary insurer has a duty to policyholders to invest wisely (to meet liabilities), and a duty to shareholders to sustain and enhance returns on assets over and above those retained to meet minimum solvency capital requirements. This means investing some free assets in long-term equities (domestic and overseas) and possibly direct property if the portfolio scale permits a diversified portfolio.
The existence of free assets enables a freer investment strategy; the extent of that freedom depends on the actual level of free assets relative to statutory solvency requirements.

sections.title: Estimating the Investment Income in Future Time Periods

subsections.title: Fixed-Interest Securities
bullets: Ignoring sale and default risk, the stream of income and redemption proceeds from fixed-interest securities is of known amount and term.

Where default risk is non-zero, an appropriate allowance must be made, e.g. decreasing amounts by 0.5% in year 1, 1% in year 2, 1.5% in year 3, 2% in year 4, etc. The reduction factor depends on the average quality of the non-gilt portfolio.
Some gilts have a spread of redemption dates at the government's option, but these are unlikely to form a significant proportion of an insurer's portfolio.

subsections.title: Index-Linked Securities
bullets: Similar to fixed-interest securities, except that payments are increased by an estimate of changes in the relevant index (e.g. RPI for index-linked gilts).

subsections.title: Equities
bullets: The current income stream from an equity portfolio is largely known and forms the basis for estimates of future income. The normal approach is to take the current income stream and assume year-on-year increases reflecting the likely path of overall dividend increases, linked to inflation and economic growth prospects.

Treating equities purely as an income stream assumes none of the equity portfolio is ever realised. This may be appropriate if equities back free assets and the liability outgo would be entirely covered by income from other assets and redemption proceeds.
If some equity holdings will need to be sold to meet liability outgo, assumptions about future market values of equities at those dates will be needed.
Input from economists and investment managers is useful when framing assumptions about equity behaviour over the next few years.

exam_qa.q: Under what circumstances might it be acceptable to treat all equity holdings as providing an infinite stream of dividends?
exam_qa.a: If the value of equities is less than the free assets (i.e. equities are backing free assets not liabilities), and the liability outgo would be entirely covered by income from other assets plus redemption proceeds of other assets such as index-linked securities, then equities would never need to be realised to meet liability outgo.

subsections.title: Property
bullets: Similar to equities: project rental income and growth rather than dividend income and growth. For small portfolios, individual lease terms and rent review dates may be modelled.
When allowing for realisation, very conservative assumptions may be used to reflect property's lack of marketability.
Investment expenses for a property portfolio are likely to be much greater than for equities; an allowance for obsolescence or periodic refitting should also be built in.

subsections.title: Cash and Other Money-Market Investments
bullets: Three approaches depending on the insurer's situation and purpose: (1) Income stream — an infinite stream based on short-term interest rates; short-term rates can vary considerably so the market's implied expected future rates (from the yield curve shape) may be used. (2) Lump sum — a lump sum cash inflow in the first time period(s), most suitable if cash is held to meet claims within the next few months. (3) Combination — cash notionally used to meet immediate liability outgo; balance rolled up at short-term interest rates.

subsections.title: Overseas Investments
bullets: Treated similarly to corresponding domestic assets; additional assumptions regarding future exchange rates are needed, consistent with those used in liability outgo projections. There may be a greater risk of default in some cases.

sections.title: Volatility of Capital Values and Investment Income for Different Asset Types

subsections.title: Fixed-Interest Securities
bullets: Income: fixed in monetary terms — no volatility unless a borrower defaults.

Capital value: depends on outstanding term. Very short-dated bonds have very little volatility (limiting case: money-market investments with near-zero volatility). Long-dated bonds can be quite volatile — e.g. if gross redemption yields rise from 6% to 9%, long-dated bond values fall by around 30%; a fall from 10% to 7% adds about 30% to value.

subsections.title: Index-Linked Gilts
bullets: Income: not generally very volatile, though uncertain. E.g. with inflation sequence 3%, 8%, 14%, 3%, 9%, the income stream starting at $100 would be approximately: 100, 103, 111, 127, 131, 142 — different each year but not highly volatile.
Capital value: usually more volatile than fixed-interest gilts of equivalent term, in both real and monetary terms.

subsections.title: Equities
bullets: Income: dividends paid by companies. From a well-diversified portfolio, the income stream is fairly stable — directors generally aim to maintain or increase dividend payments; problem companies are diversified away.

Capital value: potentially very volatile — market prices are fixed by supply and demand and can be heavily influenced by investors' emotions (confidence, fear, greed), sometimes moving to irrationally high or low levels.

subsections.title: Property
bullets: Income: from an individual property, the short-term rent is fairly certain (fixed for lease terms of, say, five years); uncertainty arises at rent review; possibility of zero rent (tenant default, vacant property). From a diversified portfolio, the rental stream is not likely to be very volatile.

Capital value: lack of price data (far fewer transactions) and heterogeneity make values very difficult to assess — estimated periodically by surveyors with significant subjectivity. Not generally as volatile on a day-to-day basis as equities, but can be fairly volatile over an extended period.

subsections.title: Cash and Other Money-Market Investments
bullets: Income: very close to prevailing short-term interest rates; can change significantly from year to year.
Capital value: virtually no volatility.

subsections.title: Overseas Investments
bullets: Any of the above categories can be held overseas, with broadly similar income and capital volatility to equivalent domestic assets; currency fluctuations cause additional volatility in domestic currency (sterling) terms.

sections.title: Suitability of Particular Types of Assets

subsections.title: Asset Features Affecting Suitability
bullets: The suitability of asset types for a health and care insurer depends on asset features including: term; currency; nature (real or fixed); expected return; security; volatility; marketability; expertise required; size of the portfolio; size of the free assets; tax position of the company; admissibility.

Main asset types the examiners expect candidates to discuss in relation to specific classes of business and free assets: ordinary shares and preference shares; government bonds and other fixed-interest securities; index-linked securities; direct property investments; cash and other money-market investments; overseas investments.

key_terms.definition: The extent to which a particular asset can be counted towards regulatory capital or solvency requirements; inadmissible assets may be held but do not count for solvency purposes.

18.summary:
Investment principles
• To maximise investment return, subject to meeting all contractual and TCF obligations and recognising the uncertainties involved and the overall risk that shareholders (of proprietary companies), regulators and policyholders will tolerate.

Asset-liability management
• Main aim is to determine a suitable investment strategy.
• Can investigate asset-liability matching requirements using deterministic or stochastic model office projections.

The effect of liabilities
• Assets chosen that are appropriate for the liabilities, ie assets should have similar term to the liabilities and respond to background influences (eg inflation and currency movements) in the same way that the liabilities do.
• The uncertainty underlying the timing of liability cashflows means that insurers need a high level of marketability for many of their assets.

ALM process
• Ease of matching depends on the type of business involved.
• Involves a liability projection system and an asset model.
• An economic scenario generator (ESG) is a model that produces numerous simulations of possible future economic outcomes, used as an input to the stochastic projection model.

Derivatives
• Used as an integral part of a company's asset-liability strategies, typically to protect against a severe adverse scenario that only arises in limited circumstances.

The effect of free assets
• Large free assets allow a more risky investment policy, eg more volatile assets, fewer 'secure assets', mismatching by term, nature and currency.

Future investment income and capital values
• Asset proceeds projections performed separately for each asset class: fixed-interest and index-linked securities, equities and property. Cash assumed as an infinite income stream, an initial lump sum inflow or a combination.
• Consider the volatility in the capital values and income from assets and how volatility differs between asset types.
• Projections of assets and liabilities useful when the relationship between the two is looked at. Either a deterministic or a stochastic model could be used.
• Asset-liability models can incorporate an assessment of the likely level of solvency at each period in the future.

20.title: Analysis of Surplus, Embedded Value and Profit

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter follows from Chapter 19 (analysis of experience) and introduces three tools for analysing company performance: analysis of surplus (for long-term business such as CI), analysis of embedded value (for long-term business), and analysis of profit (for short-term business such as PMI).
The chapter also considers customer value as a measure of profitability.

subsections.title: Purpose and Definition
bullets: An analysis of surplus provides a reconciliation between the start-year and end-year supervisory surplus, breaking the change down into contributory factors.
Surplus is defined as assets minus liabilities. 'Surplus arising' over a period is the change in the amount of surplus during that time — this can be positive or negative.
The analysis is an attempt to quantify the various elements that have caused the total change in surplus. It is better described as an 'analysis of surplus arising'.

A complication is 'interaction terms' — for example, where expenses are lower than assumed and investment returns are higher. An element of surplus arises from earning excess return on assets that were not 'supposed' to be there; this could be attributed to either expense surplus or investment return surplus. There is no uniquely correct approach, so a systematic method (Section 3) is used to ensure interaction terms are neither missed nor double-counted.

Reasons to perform an analysis of surplus: show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience; determine which assumptions are most financially significant; show the financial effect of writing new business; validate calculations and assumptions; provide a check on valuation data and process; identify non-recurring components; reconcile values for successive years; provide management information; provide data for executive remuneration schemes; provide information for publication; demonstrate that the variance in individual sources is a complete description of the variance in the total; give information on trends for the actuarial control cycle; comply with regulatory requirements (e.g. under Solvency II, profit and loss attribution is an internal model approval requirement).

key_terms.definition: Assets minus liabilities under the applicable supervisory (regulatory) valuation basis.
An element of surplus that arises as a combined effect of two or more sources of variance, making it ambiguous which single source it should be attributed to.
A form of analysis of surplus required under Solvency II as part of the internal model approval process, attributing changes in financial position to their sources.

exam_qa.q: Why might an insurance company want to perform an analysis of surplus?
exam_qa.a: To show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience; determine the most financially significant assumptions; show the effect of new business; validate calculations and assumptions; provide a check on data and process; identify non-recurring components to inform distribution decisions; reconcile successive years' values; provide management information and executive remuneration data; provide publication information; demonstrate completeness of the variance description; give trend information for the actuarial control cycle; and comply with regulatory requirements such as Solvency II P&L attribution.

subsections.title: The Sources of Surplus
bullets: Surplus arises as a result of differences between what was expected to happen (per the valuation assumptions, including implicit assumptions such as no new business) and what actually happened. Any change in the valuation assumptions themselves will also give rise to positive or negative surplus.
Possible components of the analysis of a simplified supervisory surplus (assets minus liabilities) are as follows.

Opening adjustments usually reflect changes to the model or data used to calculate the surplus, including corrections, changes to model point groupings, and methodology changes (e.g. changes to economic scenario generators or proxy modelling approaches).

Return on opening surplus results from investment returns on the opening surplus. This may be expressed as the actual return, or as the expected (risk-free) return only, with any actual difference recorded in the next item. For example, a surplus of 200 at the start of the year with 0.5% risk-free return and 1.0% actual return gives an 'expected' return of 1 and a 'difference between actual and expected' of 11 (applied to total assets), or alternatively 2 of 'actual return on surplus' and 10 of 'excess return on BEL-backing assets'.

Economic variances result from differences between actual and expected investment returns, inflation, and currency exchange rates. These will usually be the major source of variation. They include mismatch profits or losses where assets backing liabilities do not move in line with the liabilities following changes in economic conditions.
